The structured protection ETF keeps money inside the fund, growing tax-deferred through the outcome period. When the investor sells after holding longer than a year, the gain is taxed at long-term capital gains rates rather than ordinary income. For high-bracket investors, that differential is substantial.

How the Cap and Protection Move Mid-Period

The 9.81% upside cap applies to investors who buy in at the inception of the outcome period. For those entering partway through the year, the remaining cap and remaining protection adjust dynamically based on where the net asset value sits relative to its starting price. If the S&P 500 is up 5% midway through and the fund's net asset value has risen roughly 2%, a new investor buying in at that point would face a 7% remaining cap—because 7% of upside remains before hitting the original cap level—and would have approximately 98% protection still in place, since the net asset value is 2% above its starting point. Conversely, if the market has pulled back and the net asset value has dipped slightly (Kaufman notes the protected structure tends to move far less than the underlying index in a drawdown), the effective cap for a new investor increases, since the net asset value has further to appreciate before reaching the cap.

Notable Insights

"The capital protected space, as we would call it outside of ETF world, is about four times the size of the buffered space, and so it's a massive industry that has really not been disrupted by the ETF vehicle. And so that's what we're doing at Calamos."

"The money stays inside the fund so it grows and compounds tax deferred, and then if you've held it for longer than a year when you go to sell, you're paying long-term capital gains rates. So it's a huge tax alpha trade."

"What we're doing is we're buying an option package instead...we've essentially replicated our full upside and downside exposure to the market with a one-year expiration. The second layer is your protection layer: we're going to buy an at-the-money put to give you full downside protection."

Key Takeaways

  • The Calamos structured alt protection ETF delivers 100% downside protection with capped upside exposure to the S&P 500 over a one-year outcome period—a structure enabled by a higher-rate environment that wasn't viable when rates were near zero.
  • Three options positions underlie the strategy: a zero-strike call for market exposure, an at-the-money put for protection, and a sold out-of-the-money call to finance the cost. The sold call's strike sets the upside cap.
  • The upside cap resets annually when the fund rolls into a new outcome period; the 9.81% launch cap reflects option prices on May 1, 2024.
  • The ETF structure provides a significant tax advantage over CDs and annuities—gains compound inside the fund and are ultimately taxed at long-term capital gains rates rather than as ordinary income.
  • For mid-period buyers, the effective cap and remaining protection level adjust dynamically based on where the net asset value stands relative to its inception price.
  • Calamos views the capital protection space—currently dominated by fixed index annuities and principal-protected structured notes—as a multi-trillion dollar market that the ETF wrapper has not meaningfully disrupted yet.

What This Means for Advisors

The advisor use case is direct: clients sitting in CDs or money markets who want equity participation without the exposure to full drawdown risk. Kaufman runs the comparison explicitly—the after-tax return on a 5% CD is closer to 3.2% for high-bracket clients, while this product offers the chance to earn S&P 500 upside to the cap level with no greater downside risk over the outcome period. For advisors looking to reduce equity risk without extending bond duration—particularly relevant if rate volatility hasn't fully resolved—a defined protection structure provides a clear alternative to simply moving further down the fixed income spectrum.

The annual reset requires advisors to manage entry points thoughtfully. Buying near the start gives investors the full protection floor and maximum cap; buying mid-period means working with the remaining cap and adjusted protection level. Kaufman's explanation of how net asset value moves far less than the index in a drawdown gives advisors the framework to set appropriate client expectations.

The full conversation with Matt Kaufman is available on Behind the Ticker on Spotify and wherever podcasts are available.

title: Jason Hsu on Rayliant and Japan: Finding Growth in the Market Nobody Covers
metaDescription: Jason Hsu of Rayliant shares how a quantamental approach and a Sumitomo partnership unlock Japan's overlooked small and mid-cap growth market.
content:
When Brad sat down with Jason Hsu of Rayliant Global Advisors in April 2024, the thesis was clear from the start: Japan's small and mid-cap market is cheap, globally under-covered, and sitting at an inflection point most advisors haven't positioned for yet. Hsu—who co-founded Research Affiliates before spinning out Rayliant in 2016—brings both academic rigor and decades of institutional money management to a concentrated 30-name active Japan strategy built around growth, not yield.

About Jason Hsu and Rayliant

Jason Hsu started in academia before deciding he wanted to test whether the things he was teaching actually worked in practice. He co-founded Research Affiliates with Rob Arnott, developing the fundamental indexing methodology—often called RAFI—that became a reference point for the smart beta industry. In 2016, he spun out of Research Affiliates, taking over what had been the firm's Asia-focused practice and building Rayliant Global Advisors from that platform.

Today, Rayliant's primary client base is institutional: the firm manages assets for the governments of Japan, Taiwan, and Canada, as well as pension funds and sovereign wealth funds. The firm has been expanding its active ETF lineup to make strategies previously available only to institutions accessible in the wealth channel. Current ETF offerings span emerging markets broadly, emerging markets ex-China, China, and now Japan.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Rayliant's Japan equity ETF is a concentrated, actively managed portfolio of approximately 30 names focused primarily on small and mid-cap Japanese companies—the segment Hsu describes as largely uncovered by global analysts and representing genuine growth potential as Japan moves away from its long stretch of stagnation.

The strategy is built on three screening dimensions. The first is fundamental quality: growth-oriented companies with attractive valuations, targeting what Hsu calls "growth at a reasonable price." The second is corporate insider activity—what can be inferred from insider buying, governance structure, and signs of earnings management or manipulation. The third is market sentiment: Japan's equity market has unusually high retail participation, approximately 30 to 35% of trades, and Rayliant uses machine learning to process news, headlines, and tick-by-tick flows to separate retail-driven price movements from institutional conviction.

Weighting anchors heavily on fundamentals—Hsu says that bucket carries roughly two-thirds of the total weight. Sentiment functions as a catalyst, not a foundation: the team wants to identify companies with the right fundamental characteristics that are closer to their return inflection point, not value traps that remain undiscovered for years. The fund is truly active with no fixed rebalance schedule; trades occur whenever new data warrants.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

Quantamental, Not Black Box

Hsu is careful to distinguish his quantitative approach from a black box. His core thesis is that the best systematic investing tries to replicate what an experienced, disciplined stock picker actually does—but applied at scale, and without the emotional and cognitive biases that affect human judgment. The human overlay in Rayliant's Japan strategy is not there to override the model; it is there to guard-rail it. The human's role is to make sure trades and positions can be mapped back to the underlying investment thesis. If a position can't be explained in terms the fundamental framework supports, it shouldn't be there—regardless of what the model says.

Why Japan, Why Now

Hsu makes both a cyclical and structural case for Japan. Cyclically, valuations have been depressed for a long time—Japan has behaved like an emerging market, perpetually cheap but seemingly unable to convert cheapness into returns. The structural turn, he argues, is now underway: an ultra-cheap yen, inflation returning to the economy after decades of deflation, and the beginning of interest rate normalization mean that Japanese households are finally earning meaningful income on deposits and savings. This is a distinct macro environment from Europe, where the UK is still dealing with the aftermath of Brexit and the Eurozone is working through its own rate-driven pressures. Japan actually wants inflation; most of Europe is still trying to manage the consequences of fighting it. Within his international developed allocation, Hsu would consider overweighting Japan toward 50% of the sleeve—at the expense of European exposure.

The Sumitomo Partnership and Local Market Access

The strategy was built in partnership with the Sumitomo Group, one of Japan's largest financial conglomerates spanning insurance, banking, and investment management. Sumitomo's portfolio managers bring deep fundamental research on Japanese companies—particularly smaller names—that simply doesn't exist in global sell-side coverage. The combination Hsu describes is Rayliant's quantitative scoring applied alongside Sumitomo's fundamental research, producing a final portfolio neither party could construct alone. This partnership is part of why the strategy has genuine access to the small and mid-cap segment rather than simply tilting an index toward it.

Notable Insights

"The primary weighting decisions are driven by the fundamental bucket—that has the largest weight, let's say two-thirds of the weight—because you want to anchor the portfolio in something you can believe in, that you want to hold for the next five or ten years. We will never get into a company that has poor fundamentals just because the sentiment is positive, because that's a bit of a Ponzi scheme."

"Guard rails is the perfect word for it, because what you don't want is the human to come in and say, oh, I disagree with the machine, I have some other insights I want to implement—because the thesis is we believe in the human insight and the human framework, and we have done as much as we can using models to replicate that."

"Japan has for a very long time behaved like an emerging market—it's always fairly cheap...it's cheap, it's a cyclical bottom, but the growth catalysts seem so obvious with the really ultra-cheap yen and with finally inflation coming back to that economy."

Key Takeaways

  • Rayliant's Japan equity strategy focuses on the small and mid-cap segment—a part of the market largely uncovered by global analysts and dominated by growth-oriented names that don't appear in standard benchmark products.
  • Three distinct screening dimensions drive the process: fundamental quality, corporate insider signals, and machine-learning-derived sentiment from retail and institutional flows.
  • Fundamentals carry roughly two-thirds of the portfolio weight; sentiment serves as a catalyst for timing, not the primary investment rationale.
  • The strategy is built in partnership with the Sumitomo Group, providing access to deep fundamental research on Japanese companies that doesn't exist in global sell-side coverage.
  • Hsu's quantamental framework uses systematic methods to replicate disciplined human stock-picking, with human oversight functioning as guard rails against unexplainable positions—not as a competing investment view.
  • Given the relative macro setup—cheap valuation, returning inflation, normalizing rates—Rayliant recommends advisors consider a meaningful overweight to Japan within international developed allocations, potentially bringing it toward 50% of that sleeve.

What This Means for Advisors

Most advisors with international exposure hold products where European names dominate by market weight. Hsu makes a pointed argument that this default weighting may not reflect where the better opportunity sits today. Japan's macro backdrop is distinctly different from Europe's, and the small and mid-cap growth segment is almost entirely inaccessible through passive Japan exposure—the major passive products are dominated by large-cap conglomerates that have delivered modest returns for years. A dedicated active Japan allocation gives advisors a differentiated way to express an international thesis without simply adding more European cyclical exposure.

The quantamental approach also gives this strategy a more explainable story than pure quant or pure fundamental. Advisors can understand and walk clients through the investment logic: we want growth at a reasonable price, we look at what insiders are doing, and we use data science to avoid chasing retail-driven moves. The Sumitomo partnership adds a layer of local market credibility that no purely model-driven approach can replicate.

The full conversation with Jason Hsu is available on Behind the Ticker, wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Greg Reid on Building a Double-Digit Yield: Midstream Energy Meets Covered Calls
metaDescription: Greg Reid of Westwood explains how the enhanced midstream income ETF targets a double-digit yield by combining high-yield energy stocks with covered calls.
content:
The Westwood enhanced midstream income ETF launched in April 2024 as the first product in the market to combine high-yielding midstream energy stocks with a systematic covered call strategy. On this episode of Behind the Ticker, Brad sat down with Greg Reid, president of real assets at Westwood, to walk through exactly how the fund is structured, why the covered call layer changes the risk-return profile, and what a 17-year track record managing midstream energy means for portfolio construction.

About Greg Reid and Westwood

Greg Reid has been in the investment business since 1987, most of that time focused on income-generating real assets in Houston. He runs Westwood's energy team—a six-person group managing approximately $2.3 billion in assets. Westwood itself is a publicly traded firm based in Dallas, about 40 years old, that acquired Salient roughly a year and a half before this episode, bringing Reid's energy team into a firm with $16 billion in total assets under management.

The historical core of Westwood is value equities—large-cap, mid-cap, and small-cap value managed out of Dallas, representing about $8 billion of the firm's assets. The Salient acquisition added Reid's energy expertise and a tactical asset allocation business called Broadmark, along with a multi-asset class business run by Reid's partner Adrian Hord. Westwood also manages roughly $3 billion in wealth through a Texas-focused trust company business.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The Westwood enhanced midstream income ETF is actively managed, holding 20 to 22 high-yielding midstream energy stocks drawn from the Alerian Midstream Energy Select Index universe. The fund was seeded a full year before launch to prove out the strategy. The portfolio yield sits at approximately 6.3% before fees—achieved by overweighting higher-yielding names within the midstream space and underweighting low-yielding companies that don't fit an income-focused mandate.

The second layer is a systematic covered call program. Reid's team sells listed one- to two-month calls approximately 5% out of the money on the underlying holdings, targeting roughly 65 basis points in option yield over a 39-day window—which annualizes to a meaningful additional return stream. The result is a portfolio targeting a double-digit dividend yield from two separate income sources: one from the underlying midstream holdings and one from option premium. Writing covered calls also reduces portfolio beta by 10 to 15%, lowering risk alongside the yield increase.

The fund operates as a RIC (regulated investment company), which caps MLP ownership at 25% of the portfolio and requires staying below a 50% bad asset threshold. Reid's team manages both constraints deliberately—running at approximately 24.8% MLP exposure and 49% bad assets by design—skills sharpened over 12 years managing the firm's related mutual fund.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

Portfolio Construction: Income First, Then Return

Reid draws a clear distinction between the ETF and the firm's existing mutual fund, which has been running since 2012 with approximately $968 million in assets. The mutual fund targets total return—yield plus dividend growth and capital appreciation. The ETF prioritizes income first. This shifts stock selection: the ETF overweights the 7% and 8% yielders and avoids names paying 1% to 2.5%, like some LNG companies that simply don't fit an income mandate. Both strategies share the same benchmark, but the ETF's 20 to 22 names reflect deliberate concentration around high-conviction income generators.

Where Alpha Actually Comes From

When Brad asked how weighting decisions get made, Reid's answer was direct: he believes more alpha comes from how much weight you assign a name than from whether you own it at all. The team runs proprietary models with 12-month return views on every name in the universe, checks those against sell-side consensus, looks for outliers, and then allocates accordingly. Energy Transfer, for instance, represents just under 10% of the benchmark index, but Reid views it as one of the cheapest MLPs in the space—so the fund runs it at the maximum single-name position. Lower-conviction names near the bottom of the benchmark get underweighted or excluded entirely.

The ETF Wrapper's Tax Advantage for Covered Call Strategies

One underappreciated point Reid raises is that the ETF structure is especially well-suited for running covered calls at scale. In separate managed accounts or mutual funds, managing low-basis shares that get called away is operationally difficult and tax-costly. In an ETF, the team can use in-kind basket creations to remove low-basis positions before they get called, and can harvest losses systematically throughout the year. Running the same strategy in another wrapper, Reid argues, would sacrifice a meaningful portion of the tax efficiency that makes a double-digit yield genuinely attractive on an after-tax basis.

Notable Insights

"We're harvesting the volatility from the option market and we're harvesting the income from midstream and turns out we're looking at 6% from both pieces and so those two numbers kind of let us deliver a double-digit dividend yield to our ETF investors."

"Taking the yield doubling the potential yield from where it's a long only portfolio and it also lowers the risk—when you write covered calls the beta gets reduced by 10 to 15%—and so the combination of lower risk and higher yield is very attractive for many investors."

"We didn't want to be a me too product. We are the first to market with an energy ETF with covered calls. We've noticed there's been a lot of growth in covered call writing and investors obviously like it—and so we thought marrying our 6% yielding portfolio in midstream with covered call writing can get us to a double-digit dividend yield."

Key Takeaways

  • Westwood's enhanced midstream income ETF is the first product to combine high-yield midstream energy stocks with a systematic covered call overlay.
  • The double-digit yield target is built from two roughly equal streams: approximately 6% from the underlying holdings and approximately 6% annualized from option premium.
  • Writing calls 5% out of the money reduces portfolio beta by 10 to 15%, providing a lower-risk profile than a long-only midstream exposure.
  • Active weighting—not just stock selection—is where Reid believes most alpha in the strategy originates.
  • RIC structuring caps MLP ownership at 25% and requires staying below 50% bad assets; Westwood has navigated these limits across 12 years of managing the related mutual fund.
  • The ETF wrapper provides structural tax advantages over SMAs and mutual funds for covered call strategies, particularly around low-basis shares that might otherwise trigger gains when called away.

What This Means for Advisors

For clients in or approaching retirement running traditional balanced portfolios, the Westwood enhanced midstream income strategy offers a differentiated income solution. The double-digit yield target is rare in any wrapper; the systematic structure means it doesn't depend on a manager's ability to time option writes or make opportunistic trades—it runs the same discipline month after month regardless of market conditions. The covered call overlay's reduction in portfolio beta also provides a meaningful cushion relative to straight long-only midstream exposure.

The primary use case Reid describes is replacing or supplementing a portion of a balanced allocation—giving retired or income-focused investors a way to generate meaningful yield without reaching further into credit risk or duration. Because Westwood has been running related strategies since 2007 and the mutual fund version since 2012, advisors can evaluate a real performance record—not just a backtest. The ETF structure then adds the tax efficiency layer that makes the income even more attractive on a net basis.

Catch the full conversation with Greg Reid on Behind the Ticker, available on Spotify and wherever you listen to podcasts.

title: Kim Mayer on GMO's Quality ETF: When Quantitative Screens Meet Fundamental Judgment
metaDescription: GMO's Kim Mayer on combining quantitative screens with fundamental judgment to build a quality-plus-valuation ETF for the wealth channel.
content:
GMO is known for Jeremy Grantham's long-term market warnings, a valuation-first philosophy, and historically institutional-only access with minimums that once started at $10 million. When the firm launched its US quality ETF in 2023, the same quality-plus-valuation discipline GMO has run for two decades became accessible by buying a single share. Kim Mayer, a portfolio strategist and stock analyst on GMO's focused equity team, joined Brad on Behind the Ticker to walk through how the strategy is constructed, what distinguishes it from passive quality factor products, and how the team navigated both the 2022 value-driven environment and the 2023 growth rebound.

About Kim Mayer and GMO

Kim Mayer spent the first half of his career on the sell side at Morgan Stanley, starting in investment banking before moving into capital markets. He joined GMO in 2006, initially on the client side before transitioning to the focused equity team roughly 15 years ago. He wears two hats: portfolio strategist and stock analyst. His coverage area tends toward what he called "frivolity"—beer and spirits, luxury goods, TJX, and catering.

GMO was founded roughly 45 years ago by Jeremy Grantham and has historically served institutional clients with a valuation-oriented, long-term investment approach. The quality strategy has been running in some form for 20 years. In the early days of that strategy, the minimum investment was $10 million. The firm has spent the past several years deliberately broadening access to the wealth channel—moving from institutions to mutual funds and separately managed accounts, and then to the 2023 ETF launch. Mayer described the progression as a natural evolution, not a departure from the firm's philosophy but an extension of it to a broader audience, one share at a time.

Investment Strategy and Approach

GMO's quality strategy blends quantitative screening with fundamental research—and Mayer was explicit that it is neither a pure quant product nor a purely fundamental one. The process has two distinct jobs. The first is building the best quality universe the team can. A quantitative model serves as the initial screen, but the team does substantial fundamental work on top of it to catch what Mayer called false positives (non-quality companies that pass the model) and false negatives (genuine quality companies the model misses). The result is a vetted quality universe of roughly 100 names.

The second job is selecting stocks within that universe. The team applies valuation work to narrow the portfolio to approximately 60 to 70 names, then runs risk-adjustment around liquidity, diversification, and policy risks across the portfolio. The final portfolio holds roughly 40 names—concentrated enough to reflect conviction, diversified enough to avoid concentration risk in any single position. Position sizing follows a straightforward heuristic Mayer described as quality times value equals position size. Top-tier quality companies at attractive valuations can be sized up to 6% at purchase. Mid-tier quality companies have a maximum position of 3%. The top 10 positions represent roughly 40% of the portfolio. The strategy is built without benchmark constraints—the team builds sector concentrations where it finds high-quality, well-valued companies and holds nothing in sectors where it doesn't. That includes materials and real estate, which the portfolio essentially avoids—a meaningful difference from passive quality factor products that are forced into sector-neutral construction.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

On Quality Plus Valuation as the Core Differentiator

Throughout the conversation, Mayer returned repeatedly to the same argument: quality alone is not enough. People will tell you that quality wins over time, he acknowledged—but combining quality with valuation discipline does substantially better, both in terms of returns across different market periods and in terms of downside protection. This is what GMO means when it says valuation is every bit as central to the firm's DNA as quality. The passive quality factor products that advisors commonly use in model portfolios run quantitative screens and then construct sector-neutrally—which means they are forced to own the highest-quality real estate company, the highest-quality materials company, even in sectors where quality is genuinely scarce. GMO's willingness to hold nothing in those sectors is only possible because it operates without a benchmark constraint.

On Navigating 2022 and 2023

One of the more concrete examples of how the strategy operates came from Mayer's description of positioning shifts over the past few years. By 2020 and 2021, the team was actively trimming from what it described as highly consensus quality-growth tech names—companies that had become fully valued—and rotating toward what GMO calls core quality and quality value. That positioning set the portfolio up well for 2022, which was a value-driven year. Then, midway through 2022, valuation opportunities reopened in some of the same growth-oriented quality names the team had been trimming. The team shifted weight back in that direction, which in turn positioned the portfolio for the growth rebound that defined 2023. By the time of this conversation, some of those same names had gotten expensive again—and trimming had resumed. The portfolio's turnover is low, but the direction of travel is continuously driven by where valuation opportunities open and close within the quality universe.

On the Stickiness of Quality Companies

Mayer made an observation that explains why the portfolio can stay concentrated without high churn: it is genuinely difficult to become a quality company, and it is almost equally difficult to stop being one. The most common exit from the quality universe runs through poor capital allocation decisions—gradual and visible before it is complete. The practical result is that most portfolio turnover comes from valuation rebalancing rather than wholesale changes to the quality universe. The team spends most of its trading activity adjusting weights in companies it already knows well, rather than reunderwriting the portfolio from scratch.

Notable Insights

"What our research shows you is you can do substantially better in terms of delivering returns across periods—and can actually pretty significantly increase that downside protection—by combining quality and valuation."

"It's hard to become a quality company. It's almost equally hard to unbecome a quality company. Usually the way you do that is you make some bad capital allocation decisions."

"We follow a pretty simple position sizing heuristic—quality times value equals position size. We try to keep this simple, we try not to make our process overly complex."

Key Takeaways

  • GMO has managed a quality-plus-valuation strategy for 20 years; the 2023 ETF launch made it accessible at the price of a single share, down from a $10 million institutional minimum
  • The process combines a quantitative screen with fundamental vetting to build a quality universe of roughly 100 names, then applies valuation work to arrive at approximately 40 holdings
  • Position sizing is driven by a straightforward formula—quality times value—with top-tier quality companies at attractive valuations eligible for up to 6% position sizes at purchase
  • The portfolio is benchmark-agnostic and currently holds no meaningful exposure to materials or real estate—a structural difference from passive quality factor products that are constrained to sector-neutral construction
  • The team successfully navigated both the 2022 value environment and the 2023 growth rebound by adjusting valuation-driven tilts within the quality universe rather than changing the quality universe itself
  • Low turnover reflects the stickiness of quality companies—most trading activity is valuation rebalancing, not reunderwriting the portfolio

What This Means for Advisors

Advisors who already use passive quality factor products as a core equity sleeve will find this conversation useful for evaluating what an active quality manager adds. Mayer's clearest argument is structural: passive quality products, by virtue of sector-neutral construction, are required to own the best company in every sector—including sectors where quality is genuinely scarce. GMO's willingness to hold nothing in materials or real estate is only possible through active management. The valuation overlay is the second layer: passive quality products buy quality at any price; GMO uses valuation as a continuous signal that influenced how the strategy performed on opposite ends of the market cycle in 2022 and 2023.

For advisors evaluating active large-cap core exposure with a defined investment process, the GMO approach offers a clear framework—quality universe construction, fundamental vetting, valuation screening, and transparent position sizing rules. The institutional minimum is gone. What remains is the same discipline the firm has run for two decades, now accessible to a broader audience. Advisors working with clients concerned about concentration risk in narrow markets, or seeking quality exposure without expensive growth multiples, have a direct case to make.

To hear the full conversation with Kim Mayer, listen to this episode of Behind the Ticker wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Simeon Hyman on ProShares: How Daily Options Solve the Covered Call Tradeoff
metaDescription: ProShares' Simeon Hyman on how daily covered calls solve the income-versus-upside tradeoff that plagues traditional monthly strategies.
content:
Covered call ETFs have become one of the more popular product categories in the industry—but Simeon Hyman of ProShares argues most of them come with a tradeoff investors don't fully appreciate. Traditional monthly strategies sell away upside at the start of each month; if the market rallies sharply in the first few days, the remaining upside is already gone for the next 27 or 28 days. In 2023, when the S&P 500 returned 26%, the CBOE monthly buy-write index returned just 11%—less than half. ProShares built its covered call ETF around a daily options structure to address exactly that problem. Brad sat down with Hyman at the Exchange ETF conference for a direct conversation about how daily covered calls work, what investors can expect in terms of risk and income, and where this product belongs in a portfolio.

About Simeon Hyman and ProShares

Simeon Hyman has spent approximately 30 years in asset and wealth management and has been at ProShares for more than a decade. Before joining ProShares, he served as chief investment officer of a trust company—a background that shaped how he thinks about portfolio construction and income needs across different account types. ProShares manages roughly $65 billion across a range of ETF solutions. The firm is perhaps best known for its leverage and inverse lineup, which Hyman described as valuable tools for hedging and tactical exposure. It also runs a substantial buy-and-hold business anchored by its S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats ETF—a strategy targeting companies with long histories of dividend growth. The covered call product sits alongside these as part of the firm's effort to provide solutions for specific portfolio construction needs.

Investment Strategy and Approach

A covered call is a relatively straightforward concept: own a stock or basket of stocks, sell someone else the right to participate in price appreciation above a certain level, and collect premium income for doing so. The position is covered because you own the underlying asset, so there is no uncapped exposure. The tradeoff is explicit—premium income in exchange for capped upside. What traditional monthly strategies haven't adequately solved is timing. When a market rallies sharply at the start of the month, the option written at that time has already exhausted most of its upside value, leaving the portfolio without meaningful equity participation for the remainder of the period.

ProShares built its covered call ETF around daily options to address this directly. Daily options on the S&P 500 have only existed for roughly 18 months prior to this conversation, making it genuinely new territory for ETF product design. The core mechanic: by selling a new call option each day, the portfolio gets what Hyman described as "another little nibble at that upside every morning." Each day creates a fresh opportunity to participate in equity appreciation while simultaneously generating premium income. The result is a strategy that targets the full return of the S&P 500 over time while generating monthly income—an annualized distribution rate of just over 11% at the time of recording. Under the hood, the fund holds all 500 underlying stocks and executes the daily call through a swap against the S&P 500 Daily Covered Call Index, which Hyman noted is more efficient than buying individual options while being economically equivalent to writing a daily covered call.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

On the Upside Tradeoff and What Investors Often Miss

Hyman was unusually direct about what any covered call strategy requires investors to accept. You are selling away the upside. The premium income is real—but it comes at the explicit cost of capping gains when equity markets move sharply higher. What the daily structure solves is not the fundamental tradeoff but the timing problem embedded in monthly strategies. The 2023 comparison between the S&P's 26% return and the traditional monthly strategy's 11% return was the clearest illustration Hyman offered, and he was explicit about the cause: selling the month's entire upside potential in a single transaction at the beginning of the period means a strong early rally leaves nothing on the table for the remaining weeks. Writing daily creates a new opportunity to participate each morning instead of waiting for the next cycle.

On Risk and Portfolio Placement

One of the most important points from the conversation was how Hyman positioned the risk profile. The daily covered call strategy carries equity risk—specifically, S&P 500 equity risk. He was categorical about this and used it to distinguish the product from traditional monthly covered call strategies, which he said land in "a Never Never Land halfway between stocks and bonds." The daily approach is designed to sit inside a core equity allocation and behave accordingly. For investors using this product for income—whether to cover expenses, take required minimum distributions from a rollover account, or supplement yield in a retirement portfolio—the equity volatility is part of the package. Hyman's recommendation for investors concerned about equity markets was not to seek lower-volatility equity solutions but to pair equity exposure with bonds, which he argued are now capable of doing their diversifying job again with the 10-year treasury at 4%.

On the Rate Environment and Income Demand

Hyman made an observation about the income landscape that framed the broader context for where products like this fit. Short-term rates, which had been compelling for most of 2023, were already beginning to normalize: the two-year treasury had moved nearly 1% lower in yield compared to a few months prior. For investors who had parked money in money market funds at five-plus percent, that window was closing. The need for income doesn't disappear when short rates fall—it intensifies. A strategy delivering over 11% annualized distribution while maintaining core equity exposure addresses a real demand, though Hyman was careful to frame it as an equity solution rather than a fixed income substitute. The income comes with equity volatility attached.

Notable Insights

"By selling that option every day you get another little nibble at that upside every morning, and so you can generate the income that covered call strategies are known for but you can also target the full return of the S&P 500."

"Last year in 2023 the S&P was up 26%, but a traditional monthly strategy—that index—was only up 11, less than half the upside. That is what we're solving."

"The five-and-change money markets are going to be ancient history soon. People do need income, and [this strategy] can be an important contributor to that solution."

Key Takeaways

  • Traditional monthly covered call strategies sell away upside in a single transaction at the start of each period; a strong early rally leaves no further equity participation for the remainder of the month
  • Daily options on the S&P 500 are a recent innovation—only about 18 months old at the time of recording—enabling a genuinely new ETF structure that resets upside exposure each morning
  • The strategy targets the full return of the S&P 500 over time while generating monthly distributions, with an annualized rate of just over 11% at time of recording
  • Risk is equivalent to S&P 500 equity risk—this is a core equity strategy, not a bond substitute, and should be positioned accordingly in a portfolio
  • The daily call is executed via swap against the S&P 500 Daily Covered Call Index, not through discretionary daily options selection by a portfolio manager
  • As short-term yields normalize downward, daily covered call strategies offer a way to generate meaningful income within an equity allocation without abandoning equity exposure

What This Means for Advisors

The covered call ETF market has grown quickly enough that advisors face the challenge of explaining meaningful product differences to clients who may view all covered call strategies as equivalent income plays. Hyman's framework—the monthly versus daily comparison, the BXM data from 2023, and the explicit equity risk framing—gives advisors concrete language for that conversation. The key distinction is simple: this belongs in the equity sleeve, not as a fixed income substitute. For clients who need income from their equity allocation—particularly those taking distributions from retirement accounts or facing declining short-term yield on cash—a strategy targeting full equity participation while delivering meaningful monthly income addresses a real gap. Advisors who communicate the equity volatility clearly will find this a useful addition to the product conversation as rates normalize.

To hear the full conversation with Simeon Hyman, listen to this episode of Behind the Ticker wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Dodd Kittsley on Davis Funds: Concentrated Conviction, Three Generations of Research, and the Case for Staying Selective
metaDescription: Davis Funds' Dodd Kittsley on building a 22-stock conviction portfolio, why what you don't own matters, and the case for active selectivity.
content:
Dodd Kittsley was part of the ETF industry before most people knew what an ETF was. In the late 1990s, with fewer than 32 products and less than $40 billion in industry assets, he helped launch one of the first dedicated ETF research teams on Wall Street at Morgan Stanley. At the time, people struggled to distinguish an ETF from an exchange fund or an EFT—and that confusion created an opportunity to educate. He spent years as an industry evangelist, then worked at several of the largest ETF asset managers before eventually joining Davis Funds, a three-generation investment firm founded in 1969. Brad sat down with Kittsley at the Exchange ETF conference to talk about how Davis constructs a portfolio of just 22 stocks, why benchmark agnosticism means leaving entire S&P 500 sectors empty, and what makes the Davis research approach different from the typical active manager pitch.

About Dodd Kittsley and Davis Funds

Davis Funds traces its origins to a single investor—Shelby Davis—who built a substantial fortune investing almost exclusively in equities, with a particular focus on financial stocks. His son, also named Shelby, became a prominent institutional investor before eventually launching a mutual fund at the persistent urging of clients who wanted access to the same strategy. Kittsley described the firm's entry into mutual funds as another serendipitous moment, not unlike how the ETF industry itself stumbled into broad market relevance. Davis added separately managed accounts in the 2000s, and in 2017 launched its first ETFs—completing a three-vehicle lineup that now spans mutual funds, SMAs, and ETFs. The same investment philosophy and research process runs through all three wrappers.

Kittsley came to Davis after a career built largely around ETF education and advocacy. He was involved in one of the industry's first research teams at Morgan Stanley in the late 1990s, then moved into roles at some of the largest ETF asset managers on the street. That background—seeing the ETF structure from the research side, the asset manager side, and now the investment strategy side—gives him an unusual vantage point on how products should be built and why they resonate with advisors.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Everything at Davis begins with research depth. The team's standard, as Kittsley described it, is demanding: before committing a single dollar to any position, they want to be in the top decile of knowledge of the company and its industry. That means understanding management teams, competitive dynamics, customer relationships, and sustainable advantages in a way that no quantitative screen can replicate. It is, in his framing, exactly where the case for active management is strongest. The flagship Select US Equity strategy carries just 22 to 23 holdings—a deeply concentrated portfolio built entirely on conviction, with no obligation to match benchmark sector exposures.

What that looks like in practice: Davis currently holds zero exposure to three or four entire S&P 500 sectors because the team sees more compelling opportunities in areas like financials, communication services, and healthcare. Portfolio weights are driven by conviction, not index proximity. Kittsley pushed back on the common value manager label—"We're more GARPy than anything else," he said—noting the portfolio simultaneously holds Meta and JPMorgan as evidence that growth and value exist on the same spectrum for Davis rather than as competing philosophies. Turnover is low by design. Holdings are selected as long-term compounders—businesses capable of generating and growing free cash flow over many years—not positions sized around near-term earnings surprises.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

On Exclusion as a Portfolio Decision

One of the sharper points from the conversation was how deliberately Davis thinks about what it doesn't own. Kittsley framed it directly: in an environment where money isn't free anymore and interest rates have normalized, selectivity has real consequences. Businesses that were viable when capital was cheap face a fundamentally different equation today. For Davis, the benchmark-agnostic stance isn't just a philosophical position—it's a practical one. The team will not fill a sector they cannot underwrite simply to reduce tracking error or signal diversification. The portfolio holds what the research supports, nothing more.

On Why Davis Maintains All Three Delivery Vehicles

Brad pressed Kittsley on the mutual fund question: with ETF growth as dominant as it has been, why keep a mutual fund lineup? The answer was grounded and practical. Davis describes itself as delivery vehicle agnostic, offering all three wrappers not out of inertia but to provide genuine choice. Mutual funds allow for easy dollar-cost averaging and don't require daily holdings disclosure—an advantage when the strategy holds less liquid securities. Some investors, Kittsley noted, find daily visibility into their holdings emotionally counterproductive. ETFs offer tax efficiency. SMAs enable customization. The underlying strategy is identical across all three; the wrapper depends on what fits the client.

On the Worldwide Strategy and International Exposure

The Davis Select Worldwide ETF is the firm's most unconstrained product—roughly 30 to 35 holdings with no geographic limitations. It runs the same research process as the flagship US strategy but applied globally. Currently positioned approximately 50/50 between US and international names, it's designed to hold the firm's best ideas wherever they exist. Kittsley acknowledged that international equities have been an unpopular and underperforming allocation for advisors in recent years. But he was direct: some of the world's best businesses are headquartered outside the US, and for advisors seeking non-US exposure through an active, conviction-driven manager rather than a broad passive index, the worldwide strategy offers a differentiated option built on the same first-hand research discipline.

Notable Insights

"We want to be in the top decile of knowledge of companies that we own and industries that they participate in before even thinking about investing a single dollar."

"We believe that what you don't own is as important as what you do own, particularly in this environment where money isn't free anymore, interest rates are more normal, and selectivity really matters."

"We're benchmark agnostic—it's not that we don't care about our bogey or the benchmark or the opportunity set—but we will never true up a sector that we don't own. In fact, we don't own three, four sectors in the S&P 500."

Key Takeaways

  • Davis has managed the same research-intensive, conviction-driven equity philosophy across mutual funds, SMAs, and ETFs since entering the ETF business in 2017—same process, different wrappers
  • The flagship Select US Equity strategy holds just 22 to 23 names, built purely on conviction with no obligation to fill benchmark sectors
  • Several S&P 500 sectors are currently absent from the portfolio because the team sees insufficient opportunity there relative to financials, communication services, and healthcare
  • The team targets top-decile knowledge of every company before investing—including management teams, competitors, customers, and competitive moats—as the core source of active edge
  • Portfolio turnover is deliberately low; holdings are chosen as long-term compounders, not short-cycle positions
  • The Davis Select Worldwide strategy offers approximately 30 to 35 names globally, giving advisors an unconstrained active international option driven by the same research process

What This Means for Advisors

The Davis conversation is a useful reference point for advisors trying to articulate why active management earns its fee in certain contexts. In an environment where rate normalization has made selectivity consequential again, a 22-name portfolio built on first-hand research—and willing to hold zero weight in sectors the team can't underwrite—is a materially different proposition from a quasi-active strategy that tilts slightly from an index. For advisors who use passive core equity positions and want to layer in genuine active conviction, Davis offers a long-running research track record now packaged in a modern ETF structure. The delivery vehicle flexibility across mutual funds, SMAs, and ETFs also makes it practical to introduce across different client types and account structures without changing the underlying investment strategy. Kittsley's core framing—that what you don't own matters as much as what you do—is language clients can understand and advisors can build conversations around.

To hear the full conversation with Dodd Kittsley, listen to this episode of Behind the Ticker wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Sal Esposito on Zacks ETFs: Quality Earnings, Active Management, and the Zacks+ Model
metaDescription: Sal Esposito of Zacks Investment Management breaks down two active ETFs built on earnings quality and explains the Zacks+ model portfolio solution.
content:
Sal Esposito joined Brad Roth at the Exchange ETF Conference to discuss how Zacks Investment Management applies four decades of earnings research to active ETF strategies—and how the firm packaged that research into a hybrid model portfolio solution for advisors. Esposito came to Zacks from UBS, where he worked across client services, fixed income trading, product management, and advisory before helping build out the robo advisory offering. The jump from a 50,000-person firm to a 200-person company was deliberate: he wanted to build something from the ground up. Two years in, he described himself as fully converted to the ETF world—and unconvinced that separately managed accounts make sense for most strategies unless designed specifically for tax-managed indexing.

About Sal Esposito and Zacks Investment Management

Zacks Investment Research was founded in 1978 by Len Zack, who holds a PhD from MIT. The foundational insight is that sell-side earnings estimate revisions are among the most powerful signals for predicting future stock performance—not the estimates themselves, but the direction and magnitude of changes to them. That insight became the Zacks Rank system, which has powered the firm's research arm for decades. Zacks Investment Management, the asset management subsidiary, was founded in 1992 to translate that research into investable portfolios.

By the time of the interview, Zacks Investment Management managed approximately $16 billion in assets, with the large majority in separately managed accounts. The firm also runs three mutual funds and two ETFs. The ETF business is newer but grounded in the same methodology applied to SMA portfolios for over 30 years. Esposito noted that the ETF wrapper was a natural extension for high-turnover strategies: SMA portfolios with 100% annual turnover generate significant tax friction, while the ETF structure lets the portfolio turn over freely without triggering taxable events at the investor level.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Zacks runs two distinct US equity ETF strategies. The first is a large cap fund focused on earnings consistency, holding 50 to 65 names and designed to serve as a core holding in a client portfolio. The screening process starts with the top 750 largest, most liquid US companies and filters for three criteria: at least 15 years of operating history, consistent or growing earnings across multiple market cycles, and positive sell-side analyst earnings estimates for the current and following year. The portfolio is actively managed, with a target turnover around 20% annually and trading typically occurring every two weeks to monthly. Weighting is designed to track broadly with the market without taking outsized bets on individual names—Apple appears as the top holding because it meets the criteria, but it is held at a slight underweight versus its S&P 500 weight.

The second product is a small and midcap fund with approximately 198 holdings. Rather than screening for earnings consistency, this strategy focuses on earnings estimate revisions and market anomalies in the small and midcap space. The portfolio is more actively traded and carries higher annual turnover—the kind of turnover that made the strategy difficult to run efficiently in an SMA wrapper. Esposito noted that no position exceeds about 2.1% of the portfolio, keeping the fund broadly diversified even at 198 names and avoiding the capacity constraints that come with concentrated small cap strategies.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

Why Earnings History and Estimates Are the Foundation

Esposito made a clear case for why Zacks builds its screening around earnings. He was measured about what this can and cannot do: no screen predicts the future. But earnings per share history and balance sheet quality tell you something durable about how a company manages its business through varying conditions. In an environment where many large cap stocks carry elevated multiples, Esposito's position is that investors should work with a manager who does deep earnings validation as their core competency—not as one of many factors, but as the foundational filter. The earnings consistency requirement is specifically designed to identify companies that have managed their financials across multiple market cycles, not just during favorable conditions, and the forward-looking analyst estimate screen adds a second check that the pattern is expected to continue.

The Small and Midcap Opportunity

Esposito was direct about why he believes active management has a structural advantage in small and midcap equities specifically. Small and midcap indexes include a significant number of unprofitable companies and underperformers that systematically drag down returns. At the same time, smaller companies receive far less sell-side analyst coverage than large caps, which means pricing inefficiencies persist longer and are exploitable by a team with deep systematic research capabilities. Zacks has been building that capability for nearly 50 years across both the research and investment management arms of the firm. The ETF structure is particularly well-suited to capitalizing on it because the portfolio can act quickly when opportunities emerge without generating tax consequences for shareholders at the fund level.

Zacks+ and the Hybrid Model Portfolio

One of the more distinctive parts of the conversation covered Zacks+, which is not an ETF product but a model portfolio solution that wraps the firm's active ETFs together with separately managed account sleeves holding individual stocks. The ETFs handle the core of the trading and provide the tax-efficiency of the ETF wrapper. The SMA sleeves add individual stock exposure—which Esposito noted many clients still want for the conversational value of knowing exactly what they hold. The full solution is available on Schwab and Fidelity, and Zacks does not charge a separate strategist fee on top of the ETF expense ratios. The firm runs seven fully funded models across the risk spectrum, and also builds custom solutions for advisors with specific client needs or household-level requirements.

Notable Insights

"You can't ever predict the future, but you can certainly know a lot about a company by looking at its earnings per share and its balance sheet."

"The anomalies occur in the small and midcap companies—and having a portfolio that's active, with a team looking at this daily and trading throughout the year, gives you a little bit more of an advantage versus just putting your clients in a small and midcap index fund."

"Zacks+ is kind of taking a step to a little more sophistication for a client that might want exposure to single stock securities—and you don't have to go down the road of pulling in three or four different managers doing three or four different things."

Key Takeaways

  • Zacks Investment Management was founded in 1992 as the investment arm of Zacks Investment Research, which has applied the Zacks Rank earnings revision system since 1978 and manages approximately $16 billion across SMAs, mutual funds, and ETFs.
  • The large cap earnings-consistent fund screens for 15-year operating history, consistent or growing earnings across market cycles, and positive sell-side analyst estimates—producing a 50-to-65-name portfolio designed as a core holding with roughly 20% annual turnover.
  • The small and midcap fund holds approximately 198 names and exploits earnings revision signals and market anomalies in a segment with lower analyst coverage and more persistent pricing inefficiencies.
  • The ETF structure was deliberately selected for the small and midcap strategy because it allows high portfolio turnover without generating taxable events for investors at the fund level.
  • Zacks+ combines core active ETFs with individual stock SMA sleeves across seven risk-spectrum models, available on Schwab and Fidelity with no separate strategist fee.
  • Esposito positions the large cap fund as a quality-tilted complement that can coexist with passive large cap index exposure, while the small and midcap fund is designed to replace—not sit alongside—passive small and midcap index funds.

What This Means for Advisors

Esposito made two concrete placement suggestions. The large cap earnings-consistent fund sits alongside a passive large cap core position as a quality-tilted complement—built to hold up better in down markets, not to generate outsized returns in rallies. With a three-year track record approaching, advisors have meaningful data on how it behaves. The small and midcap fund is best used as a replacement for passive index exposure in that segment, not a complement to it—active management has a genuine structural edge there that passive approaches cannot capture. For advisors running model portfolios who want a more complete solution, Zacks+ brings active ETFs, individual stock exposure, and model delivery together without adding a strategist fee layer on top.

You can hear the full conversation with Sal Esposito on Behind the Ticker, available wherever you listen to podcasts.

title: Johan Grahn on Allianz Buffered ETFs: Defined Outcomes for a Risk-Averse Market
metaDescription: Johan Grahn of Allianz Investment Management explains how buffered ETFs work and how advisors can use them across client portfolios.
content:
Johan Grahn joined Brad Roth at the Exchange ETF Conference to explain how Allianz Investment Management approaches defined outcome investing through its suite of buffered ETFs. Grahn spent more than 20 years in institutional asset management before joining Allianz, with stints at a university endowment, an investment consulting firm, and a large insurance company. That background in risk-managed strategies shapes how he talks about these products: not as exotic derivatives vehicles, but as tools that formalize the downside protection many investors already want but rarely get in a clear, liquid, daily-tradeable format. The conversation gave advisors a comprehensive look at how the products work mechanically, how they are used in practice, and where they fit in a portfolio construction context.

About Johan Grahn and Allianz Investment Management

Allianz Investment Management is wholly owned by Allianz Life, an insurance company based in Minneapolis. For decades before entering the ETF market, the firm's core competency was managing the risk associated with issuing insurance products—delivering on guarantees and defined outcomes to policyholders. That means the trading desk, portfolio managers, and quant team were trained around outcome delivery, not return maximization. Grahn noted this is precisely the skill set required to run buffered ETF strategies: you are making a promise about a range of outcomes, and you have to structure and manage the portfolio to deliver it. The firm entered the ETF space in mid-2020.

Grahn's own background traces a deliberate arc through risk management. He started at a university endowment, where he was part of a team that completely rebuilt a $250 million portfolio across equities, fixed income, hedge funds, real estate, and timber. He then joined a consulting firm advising other endowments, foundations, and pension plans on portfolio construction and manager selection. He later built portfolios for a large insurance company, working with volatility-managed strategies, risk parity, momentum overlays, and rate management. He described the core lesson from that work as learning to "squeeze the most amount of return out of the least amount of risk that you take."

Investment Strategy and Approach

Allianz's buffered ETF suite is built on options packages—specifically, Delta-adjusted Flex options, which are custom exchange-traded options contracts. Each ETF holds a put spread to establish the buffer (protection against a defined percentage of downside loss) and sells a call to fund that buffer, which creates the cap on upside participation. The underlying reference asset across the suite is the S&P 500, using the Spy ETF as the reference.

The lineup includes three primary product structures. The first is a 12-month product with a 10% buffer—if the S&P falls 10% or less over the outcome period, the investor keeps their full principal. The second is a 12-month product with a 20% buffer, offering deeper downside protection at the cost of a lower upside cap. The third is a 6-month product with a 10% buffer, which works the same way but resets every six months. A fourth product—a 6-month floor—limits investor losses to a hard floor of 5%, regardless of how far markets fall. For each 12-month series, Allianz offers 12 separate funds, one for each calendar month, so advisors can always access a fresh set of starting parameters. When an outcome period ends, the ETF resets automatically—no action required.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

How the Buffer Works Mechanically

Grahn walked through the mechanics with a concrete example. An investor puts $1 million into a fund with a 10% buffer over a 12-month period. If the market falls 10% or less, the investor keeps the million. If the market falls 11%, the investor loses 1%—the first 10% is absorbed. On the upside, if the cap is 17-18% and the market returns 30%, the investor captures only the capped amount. If the market returns 10%, the investor keeps all of it. The 20% buffer product works identically but absorbs a deeper loss in exchange for a lower cap. Grahn was clear that the options contracts themselves are set on day one and do not need to be actively traded during the outcome period—the firm buys or sells more of the same contracts as creation and redemption activity happens in the ETF.

Buying Mid-Period and What Changes

One of the more practically useful parts of the conversation covered what happens when investors buy these products after the outcome period has already started. The buffer and cap are priced into the ETF's current market price, not fixed at the day-one parameters. If the market has risen 5% since the outcome period began, a new buyer's effective entry point is already 5% closer to the cap—leaving less upside participation remaining. The buffer anchor is set in dollar terms on day one; as the ETF's price moves, a mid-period buyer's effective cushion changes accordingly. Grahn emphasized that the daily trading flexibility—versus illiquid structured products that lock investors in—is one of the product's core advantages, but advisors need to understand mid-period pricing before placing clients outside of reset dates.

The Asset Allocation Case: Beyond Equity Risk Reduction

The most pointed part of the conversation concerned how to think about these products within a broader portfolio. Most advisors consider buffered ETFs as equity risk reducers—taking some equity exposure and adding a defined buffer. That works. But Grahn made a more interesting case: replacing fixed income. His argument is structural. Rates at 5% still require investors to pay taxes on income with limited total return potential. And the traditional reason to own bonds—negative correlation to equities during downturns—has been less reliable. Meanwhile, more Americans are retiring every day and moving money out of equities into income-oriented assets. A 20% buffered equity product can provide lower volatility than equities while maintaining positive correlation when markets rise. Grahn's point was direct: in a standard 60/40 portfolio, the vast majority of risk comes from the equity side regardless of what the other 40% holds.

Notable Insights

"Let's say you put a million dollars into one of our ETFs and it comes with a 10% buffer over a 12-month outcome period—so if the market is down by 10% or less, you still keep your million dollars at the end of this outcome period, net of fees."

"The buffer isn't free—so what we do is we have a cap on how much you can make over that same 12-month outcome period."

"80 to 90% of the risk is coming from equities—that's your risk, no matter what the other half of the portfolio is."

Key Takeaways

  • Allianz Investment Management entered the ETF market in mid-2020, drawing on decades of experience managing defined outcomes for insurance policyholders—a directly relevant skill set for running buffered strategies.
  • Buffered ETFs use options packages—specifically Delta-adjusted Flex options—to establish a defined range of outcomes, with a buffer absorbing a percentage of downside and a cap limiting upside participation.
  • The suite includes 10% and 20% buffer products over 12-month periods, a 6-month 10% buffer product, and a newer 6-month floor product limiting losses to a hard 5% maximum.
  • Each 12-month series offers 12 separate funds—one per calendar month—so advisors can always access fresh starting parameters without waiting for a single annual reset.
  • Investors who buy mid-period receive different effective caps and buffers than day-one purchasers; advisors need to understand mid-period pricing before placing clients at non-reset dates.
  • These products can function as equity risk reducers, fixed income alternatives, or replacements for alternative allocations—each use case with a distinct risk/return rationale and different client profile.

What This Means for Advisors

Grahn's most useful insight for advisors is to start with the problem, not the product. For clients who are equity-heavy and approaching or entering retirement, a buffer shaves risk without abandoning market participation. For clients sitting in fixed income looking for more growth potential, a 20% buffered equity product provides market upside with meaningful downside protection and lower volatility. For clients holding alternatives that aren't delivering their expected diversification benefit, a buffered product gives a known outcome range instead of an uncertain one. Grahn also flagged the multi-month series structure as a practical tool for larger allocations—buying four different monthly versions of the same buffer product diversifies across outcome periods and further reduces the odds of a bad outcome in any single period affecting the full allocation.

You can hear the full conversation with Johan Grahn on Behind the Ticker, available wherever you listen to podcasts.

title: Weldon Rice on QRAFT AI: Building the Next Generation of AI-Driven ETFs
metaDescription: Weldon Rice of QRAFT Technologies discusses how AI and machine learning are reshaping US equity investing through two distinct active ETF strategies.
content:
Weldon Rice joined Brad Roth at the Exchange ETF Conference to walk through how QRAFT Technologies—a Korean asset management and technology company—uses artificial intelligence to build US equity portfolios. Rice heads the AI ETF division at QRAFT and came to the industry through an unconventional path: after college, he moved to Asia, spent more than a decade living in Korea, studied quantitative finance under practitioners at major institutions, and eventually joined QRAFT as it was expanding into the ETF market. The conversation covered how the firm thinks about AI versus traditional quant investing, the mechanics of its two US equity products, and how advisors might use them in practice.

About Weldon Rice and QRAFT Technologies

Weldon Rice leads the AI ETF division at QRAFT Technologies, which was founded in 2016 in Seoul as an investment technology company—not a traditional asset manager. The firm's core business is providing AI-driven portfolio construction to institutional clients, primarily large Korean financial institutions. QRAFT helps those clients build AI-powered products, robo advisory platforms, and model portfolios. Most of the firm's employees are engineers and researchers, and all AI models are developed entirely in-house, with data sourcing, cleaning, and model development fully integrated under one roof.

Two years before the interview, QRAFT received a $140 million investment from SoftBank—a milestone Rice described as transformative. The funding allowed the firm to expand into new asset classes, new geographic markets, and higher-frequency strategies. Before the investment, QRAFT had about 40 people on staff. The ETF products represent one arm of a broader platform spanning separately managed accounts, multi-asset strategies, and technology solutions for institutional partners. Rice credited Francis Oh—his boss and mentor—with building much of the ETF business after careers spanning Vanguard, Direxion, and QRAFT itself.

Investment Strategy and Approach

QRAFT runs two US equity products with different objectives and risk profiles. The first is a broadly diversified US large cap fund targeting 300 to 320 names, rebalanced monthly. The model starts with traditional academic factors—value, quality, momentum, and others—combined with QRAFT's proprietary factors. These scores feed into a second model that incorporates market and fundamental data to rank securities by expected price appreciation over the following four weeks. The result is a portfolio built for long-term, risk-adjusted outperformance through systematic short-term factor exploitation. Rice noted the fund can get more defensive in weaker markets—tilting toward value and quality factors when conditions deteriorate, and toward momentum and growth factors when markets are trending up.

The second product is a concentrated US large cap momentum fund with 50 holdings. Rice described the contrast directly: "We like to say—we're from Korea, we like spicy food—this one is a lot more spicy." With sector concentration potentially reaching 50% and individual position sizes up to 9-10%, it is explicitly a high-conviction trend-following vehicle. Like the diversified fund, it rebalances monthly, but its profile is fundamentally different—designed as a satellite holding rather than a core complement.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

AI as a Tool, Not a Theme

Rice was precise about how QRAFT positions itself. The firm does not hold AI-related stocks—it uses AI to determine which stocks to hold. That distinction matters for how advisors categorize these products. QRAFT describes its approach as "Quant 2.0": taking academically validated investment factors that systematic managers have used for decades and applying AI to create a more dynamic, robust exposure to them. The core upgrade over traditional quant models is a shift from linear to nonlinear modeling. Traditional regression-based quant systems assume markets behave predictably and mean-revert. QRAFT's models do not make that assumption—they are built to capture patterns that linear approaches would miss. Rice was also clear on large language models: QRAFT's primary approach is analytical AI focused on structured numerical data, though the firm has started incorporating sentiment-based capabilities through its partnership with LGI Research on a newer product.

Machine Learning, Deep Learning, and Continuous Updating

Rice clarified terminology that gets conflated in financial marketing. Machine learning and deep learning are both subsets of artificial intelligence, not synonyms for it. He described machine learning models as relatively more interpretable, while deep learning models are more capable of identifying complex nonlinear patterns at the cost of some transparency. QRAFT uses both. The models update continuously as new market data arrives—learning from changing conditions in real time rather than running on static historical training sets. The firm's research team is also actively evaluating new data sources and modeling approaches, so the system evolves over time.

Where Human Judgment Stops

One of the operationally significant points in the conversation concerned the boundary between human and AI decision-making at QRAFT. Humans determine the investment universe—which securities the model is allowed to consider. Humans also select the factor inputs and set risk guardrails around sector concentration. But once those inputs are established, the model's output is followed with discipline. Rice was direct: the team does not review the model's selections and override holdings that feel uncomfortable. The guardrails constrain behavior at the margin—they do not give humans discretion to second-guess the model's core outputs once the process is in motion.

Notable Insights

"We're not an AI thematic fund—we're a fund that uses AI to pick the stocks in the fund. So that's usually the first point."

"We take a lot of those academically researched factors from the quant space that have been around for years, and what we're trying to do is use AI to bring a more robust exposure to those factors."

"We let the AI learn the data and we fully trust the output that comes out from the model. We don't go, 'Oh, we're in this stock this month—really? I don't think so, let's dial that down.' We trust the process."

Key Takeaways

  • QRAFT is an engineering-first investment company founded in Korea in 2016, with most of its staff made up of researchers and developers—not traditional investment professionals.
  • The firm received $140 million in investment from SoftBank, funding expansion into new asset classes, geographies, and higher-frequency strategies.
  • The diversified US large cap fund holds 300 to 320 names and is designed as an active complement to passive large cap exposure, rebalancing monthly based on multi-factor AI models.
  • The momentum-focused fund holds 50 names with high sector and position concentration—a satellite vehicle for clients with higher risk tolerance seeking trend exposure.
  • QRAFT uses both machine learning and deep learning, and its models update continuously on incoming data rather than running on static historical training sets.
  • Human judgment defines the investment universe and risk guardrails, but once set, the AI's portfolio outputs are not subject to discretionary override.

What This Means for Advisors

Rice offered a clear implementation framework. The diversified large cap fund is positioned as a complement to a core passive position—an active, data-driven sleeve that brings different factor exposure without concentrating risk. For clients who want pure trend exposure, the momentum-focused fund offers that in a concentrated form, best suited as a satellite. Rice's broader point on diversification is worth sitting with: AI-based strategies approach market data from a fundamentally different angle than human managers do. That difference can create diversification benefits at the strategy level—not just within portfolios, but in how portfolios are constructed and managed over time. Rice's view is that AI investing works best as a complement to existing exposure, not a wholesale replacement for it.

You can hear the full conversation with Weldon Rice on Behind the Ticker, available wherever you listen to podcasts.

title: David LaValle on Grayscale: Ten Years in the Making, the Bitcoin ETF Legal Battle, and What Advisors Need to Know
metaDescription: David LaValle of Grayscale on the Bitcoin ETF approval journey, the DC Circuit court battle, and how the custody structure works.
content:
On January 11, 2024, Grayscale's Bitcoin fund began trading on NYSE Arca as an ETF. What looked from the outside like a regulatory milestone was the culmination of a decade-long promise to investors, a federal court battle, and a final sprint of industry-wide logistics that David LaValle described as gymnastics. On this episode of Behind the Ticker—recorded live at the Exchange ETF Conference—LaValle walked Brad Roth through the full arc: the legal strategy, the structural mechanics of conversion, what Bitcoin actually is, and where Grayscale's product development goes from here.

About David LaValle and Grayscale

LaValle's background spans nearly every position in the ETF ecosystem. He started on the floors of the stock exchanges, trading equities, listed derivatives, and structured notes, eventually becoming part of the largest ETF market-making operation on the floor of the American Stock Exchange. After that business was sold, he moved to NASDAQ to run its ETF listing and trading business, then to State Street, and ultimately to serve as CEO of a global indexing firm. When Grayscale's CEO cold-called him about building out an ETF franchise, his first reaction was skepticism. After learning more about the opportunity, he saw it as a chance to connect two defining innovations in finance.

Grayscale is the world's largest digital currency asset manager. Beyond its flagship Bitcoin product, the firm manages 17 digital asset products across a four-phase lifecycle: private placements that season under Rule 144, then move to OTC market quotation, then become SEC reporting companies, and finally convert to ETFs. Single-asset products cover Bitcoin, ethereum, and solana, alongside an index-based digital large-cap fund. Grayscale has also partnered with FTSE to develop a crypto sector classification framework, and the firm's product development cycle is designed to seed today what it believes will meet advisor demand in five to ten years.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Grayscale's core strategy is access—using the regulatory framework to bring exposure to digital assets to the broadest possible range of investors. LaValle characterized the firm's role as education as much as product management. The Bitcoin product was created in 2013 with an explicit long-term promise: to eventually convert it into an ETF. It was structured from the start as a 1933 Act Delaware grantor trust, the same regulatory framework used by commodity-based products like gold. That design choice meant the conversion, when it finally arrived, required only two structural changes: the listing venue (from OTC to NYSE Arca) and the addition of simultaneous creation and redemption rights. Everything else was already in place.

Deeper Dive
