The Legal Battle: From Denial to Unanimous Victory

The sequence that led to Bitcoin ETF approval began in the third quarter of 2021, when SEC Chair Gensler signaled the agency was open to Bitcoin Futures ETFs. On the first day those products began trading in October 2021, Grayscale filed to convert its Bitcoin fund from an OTC-quoted private placement to a listed ETF. A 240-day regulatory review followed, ending in a denial in late June 2022. The same day the denial arrived, Grayscale challenged the decision in the DC Circuit Court of Appeals under the Administrative Procedures Act, arguing the SEC had treated similar products differently without adequate justification.

The argument centered on a straightforward inconsistency. If the SEC was comfortable with Bitcoin Futures ETFs, Grayscale contended, it had to justify why it was not equally comfortable with Bitcoin spot exposure. Bitcoin futures are priced off bitcoin spot; approving one and refusing the other without explanation amounted to arbitrary and capricious behavior under administrative law. LaValle referenced an analogy from ETF analyst Eric Balchunas: approving a futures product but not a spot product was like saying the agency was fine with the R-rated movie but not the PG-13 version. In late August 2023, the DC Circuit issued a unanimous ruling in Grayscale's favor, finding the SEC had acted in an impermissible way. The agency had repeatedly claimed in its written briefs that Bitcoin Futures and Bitcoin spot markets were different—but when judges asked directly why, the SEC could not provide a sufficient answer.

The Industry Sprint to Launch

After the court ruling, Grayscale entered a 45-day window of uncertainty while waiting to see if the SEC would appeal. When it did not, the path to approval opened—but getting 11 spot Bitcoin ETFs to market simultaneously required coordinated effort from administrators, custodians, authorized participants, market makers, and exchanges. The two weeks before and after the Christmas holidays were particularly compressed. LaValle described the period as a significant test of the ETF industry's ability to execute. Eleven issuers and their full operational ecosystems had to be ready on the same day. As he put it, the beauty was that everyone showed up.

The Custody Architecture

Because Grayscale operates under the 1933 Act rather than the 1940 Act, the custody of Bitcoin sits outside the traditional ETF administrator structure—directly mirroring the framework established when gold ETFs first came to market. LaValle noted that when GLD launched, the gold custodian (HSBC) and the ETF share administrator (Bank of New York) were necessarily separate institutions, because the administrator did not have vaults. The Bitcoin spot products follow the same logic. Bank of New York handles ETF share administration. Coinbase handles custody of the actual Bitcoin. At the time of launch, Grayscale had $28 billion in assets already in custody at Coinbase—a long-standing operational relationship with an established NAV-striking process. For the firm, the custody mechanics were a known quantity, not an improvised solution.

Notable Insights

"We had tremendous conviction that we were going to deliver on the promise that we offered our investors—that we were going to deliver Bitcoin in an ETF wrapper—and in 2013 when this product was built it was built to mimic the GLD construct, a 1933 Act Delaware grantor trust that was going to be a building block for clients in their portfolios."

"We were all pretty much twisted up into a pretzel to make this thing happen, and the beauty of it is everybody showed up and it worked, and these products are behaving as designed."

"The question that we get asked mostly, although it started to dissipate from advisors, is why would I ever use Bitcoin to buy a coffee in the morning—and the answer is you probably wouldn't if you're in the US market, and that's where the conversation starts and that's where the education starts."

Key Takeaways

  • Grayscale's Bitcoin product was created in 2013 as a 1933 Act Delaware grantor trust specifically designed to become an ETF—the January 2024 listing was the fulfillment of a ten-year-old promise to investors
  • The legal strategy—challenging the SEC's denial in the DC Circuit Court of Appeals under the Administrative Procedures Act—was filed the same day approval was denied, and produced a unanimous ruling against the agency in August 2023
  • The SEC's central legal problem, per the court: it repeatedly asserted that Bitcoin Futures and spot markets were different in its briefs but could not explain why when judges asked directly
  • The two structural changes required at conversion were minimal—listing venue from OTC to NYSE Arca, and the addition of simultaneous creation and redemption—because the product was designed for ETF operation from the beginning
  • Custody architecture mirrors the GLD model: ETF share administration (Bank of New York) is separate from Bitcoin custody (Coinbase), which already held $28 billion of Grayscale's assets at launch
  • Grayscale maintains a four-phase lifecycle framework across 17 digital asset products, seeding new private placements today that the firm believes will meet advisor demand five to ten years from now

What This Means for Advisors

For advisors still working through client questions on Bitcoin, Grayscale's education-first posture offers a practical starting point. The firm produced a physical Bitcoin guidebook designed for advisors to keep on their desk and share with clients, available on request at info@grayscale.com. LaValle's framing—positioning Bitcoin as a portfolio building block analogous to gold or commodity exposure rather than a speculative trade—reflects how the firm is approaching the advisor conversation. Understanding the structural mechanics matters for advisor due diligence as well: the 1933 Act structure, Coinbase's role as custodian, and the process by which NAV is struck daily are all questions advisors should be prepared to answer.

Listen to the full conversation with David LaValle on Behind the Ticker, available wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Keri Riemer & Hazel Doyle of K&L Gates: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription:
Special Live From Exchange ETF Series

In this live and in-person episode of "Behind the Ticker," host Brad Roth welcomes Keri Riemer and Hazel Doyle
content:
Special Live From Exchange ETF Series

About Keri Riemer & Hazel Doyle and K&L Gates

In this live and in-person episode of "Behind the Ticker," host Brad Roth welcomes Keri Riemer and Hazel Doyle to explore the evolving landscape of Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs) from both a U.S. and European perspective. This engaging conversation offers a deep dive into the intricacies of ETFs, their legal frameworks, and the burgeoning growth within the industry.Keri Riemer, with a rich background in corporate law, asset management, and regulatory roles, including a stint at the SEC, shares her journey into the world of ETFs. She emphasizes the importance of detail-oriented and hardworking attributes that propelled her into a successful career in asset management and ETFs. Keri’s current role at K&L Gates allows her to leverage her comprehensive experience to assist clients in navigating the complex regulations governing ETFs, particularly under the Investment Company Act of 1940.Hazel Doyle, representing the K&L Gates Dublin office, brings a unique path to the legal world, from paralegal to a specialist in funds, including ETFs. Her Irish and geography background, coupled with hands-on experience in the funds industry, underscores the diverse skills and knowledge required in the evolving ETF space. Hazel highlights the growth of ETFs in Europe, noting the differences in regulatory environments and market trends compared to the U.S., with a particular emphasis on ESG investing, thematic ETFs, and the challenges and opportunities of cross-border distribution.The discussion provides valuable insights into what it takes to launch and manage ETFs, the role of attorneys in the ETF industry, and the legal and operational complexities involved. Both guests underscore the importance of innovation, patience, and thorough preparation in achieving success in the competitive ETF market. They also discuss the challenges and considerations for U.S. issuers looking to enter the European ETF market, including regulatory compliance, distribution strategies, and the need for a robust understanding of investor preferences and market dynamics.Listeners gain a comprehensive overview of the ETF landscape, including the growth drivers, the impact of regulatory frameworks in different jurisdictions, and practical advice for those considering launching or investing in ETFs. Keri and Hazel's expertise and experiences provide a rich resource for understanding the challenges and opportunities within the global ETF market.

On Process and Philosophy

I'm sorry, I'm just thinking about initiating an ETF, and I'm calling you, okay, and I'll get you some starting, just thinking about the process.

But the benefits that ETFs have has since day one, which is tax efficiency, relatively lower cost for investors, that's continued to grow since day one. I think there's been demands for AIAs to offer more ETF products to the clients. And you have portfolio and the thing but your portfolio transparency requirements. We've got the benchmark regulations. So benchmark administrators are subject to make sure that their indexes are compliant with them certain rules.

Key Takeaways

title: Adam Curran of YALL: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: Adam Curran shares his background growing up in a household focused on money and his journey to starting his own financial planning firm.
content:
Adam Curran shares his background growing up in a household focused on money and his journey to starting his own financial planning firm. He introduces the God Bless America ETF, which aims to provide conservative investors with an investment option that aligns with their values. He explains the screening process for selecting companies based on their political and social activism. Curran also discusses the weighting and selection of holdings within the ETF and the challenges of marketing the fund. He emphasizes the importance of focusing on business and returns for shareholders. Adam Curran discusses his firm Retire Y'all and the God Bless America ETF. He shares his inspiration behind creating Retire Y'all and the importance of investing in the American Dream. Curran emphasizes the need for financial education and building a community of investors. He also discusses the role of technology in investing and shares his vision for the future of Retire Y'all.

About Adam Curran and YALL

Adam Curran's background growing up in a household focused on money and his journey to starting his own financial planning firm

Today we have on Adam Carran, he is the founder of the God Bless America ETF, Ticker y'all, Y-A-L-L is a fund that focuses on US equities, however, has a filter and screen to eliminate those that are involved in political agendas and activism. title: Todd Rosenbluth of VettaFi: Inside Their Investment Approach
metaDescription: In this conversation, Brad interviews Todd Rosenbluth, an industry veteran and current member of VettaFi.
content:
In this conversation, Brad interviews Todd Rosenbluth, an industry veteran and current member of VettaFi. They discuss Todd's background, VettaFi's business, and the upcoming Exchange conference. Todd shares his insights on the future of the ETF industry and offers advice for new ETF issuers. The conversation highlights the collaborative nature of the ETF community and emphasizes the importance of ongoing engagement and communication. Todd provides information on where to learn more about VettaFi and access his research.Takeaways· VettaFi is an ETF services company that aims to educate financial advisors and provide them with content, webcasts, and data to help them make informed investment decisions. · The Exchange conference, organized by VettaFi, brings together industry experts, asset managers, and financial advisors to share ideas, discuss trends, and provide training and education. · The future of the ETF industry is expected to see continued growth in actively managed ETFs, conversions of separately managed accounts (SMAs) into ETFs, and a focus on education and collaboration within the community. · To make the most of the Exchange conference, financial advisors are encouraged to review the agenda, attend sessions that align with their interests, and take advantage of networking opportunities. · The ETF community fosters collaboration and a sense of community, with industry participants willing to help and support each other. · New ETF issuers should engage with platforms like VettaFi to get their word out and stay in front of industry experts and writers who can help promote their products. · To learn more about VettaFi and access Todd Rosenbluth's research, visit the VettaFi and ETF Trends websites, as well as connect with Todd on LinkedIn.Chapters00:00 Introduction and Background 01:35 VettaFi's Acquisition and Products 07:35 Overview of the Exchange Conference 10:09 The Future of the ETF Industry 12:12 Making the Most of the Exchange Conference 15:02 Highlighted Sessions at the Exchange Conference 19:30 Planning for the Exchange Conference 21:45 Information and Registration for the Exchange Conference 23:40 The Collaborative Nature of the ETF Community 27:13 Advice for New ETF Issuers 30:13 Where to Learn More about VettaFi and Todd Rosenbluth

title: Matt Barry on Touchstone's Active ETF Platform: Manager Due Diligence, Climate Transition, and Securitized Income
metaDescription: Matt Barry of Touchstone Investments on active ETF growth, sub-advisor due diligence, climate transition investing, and securitized income.
content:
Touchstone Investments has been a mutual fund manager for decades. Getting into ETFs was not inevitable—it was a deliberate bet on where the market was heading. On this episode of Behind the Ticker, Matt Barry, who oversees product management and ETF capital markets at Touchstone, walked Brad Roth through the firm's distinctively active philosophy, its sub-advisor selection discipline, and the two ETF strategies he has spent the most time building: a climate transition fund managed by a centuries-old Swiss firm and a securitized income strategy run by a team that has been together for more than twenty years.

About Matt Barry and Touchstone Investments

Touchstone is a $25 billion mutual fund manager that runs a fully sub-advised model. Every strategy the firm offers—across mutual funds and, more recently, ETFs—is managed by an outside institutional manager selected through a structured evaluation process. Barry joined Touchstone out of business school, worked through product management roles, and over the past several years has been the primary architect of Touchstone's ETF platform, which launched roughly 18 months before this conversation.

The firm has 15 sub-advisors across its lineup and a distribution team of more than 50 people targeting the advisor and intermediary channel. That distribution infrastructure, built around the mutual fund business, is now being applied to ETFs—though Barry acknowledged there are meaningful differences in how advisors discover and evaluate products across the two vehicles. Touchstone's core identity is captured in its own phrase: distinctively active. Strategies are built to diverge from their benchmarks. Barry explained the logic directly: to beat a benchmark, a manager first has to look different from it.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Touchstone evaluates sub-advisors through a framework it calls SPIDR, covering organizational stability, personnel, infrastructure, investment discipline, and results. The firm is not testing ideas with client capital; it requires a demonstrated track record and a repeatable investment process before a manager is hired. Equally important is the competitive landscape analysis that precedes every launch. Before bringing a strategy to market, Touchstone researches what already exists and assesses whether its chosen manager offers something genuinely differentiated.

The firm's approach to sourcing managers combines active search with opportunistic discovery. Hundreds of meetings per year with institutional asset managers create a steady flow of opportunities. Some lead to deliberate searches in specific asset classes—Touchstone hired Aries Capital to run a credit opportunities strategy in the liquid alternatives space after exactly that kind of structured search. Others result in products the firm was not originally looking for. The climate transition strategy managed by Lombard Odier fell into the second category: an incoming pitch that Touchstone found compelling enough to build around.

Deeper Dive

What Makes a Good Sub-Advisor

Barry's SPIDR framework reflects a discipline that goes beyond performance screens. Organizational stability and infrastructure continuity matter as much as recent returns. The team at Fort Washington—which manages Touchstone's securitized income strategy—has been together for more than 20 years, a signal of the institutional durability Touchstone looks for. Capacity discipline is another criterion: managers who have grown so large that they are structurally forced to mimic the benchmark are disqualified by definition. High tracking error is a feature, not a problem, at Touchstone, as long as it is the product of genuine conviction. Barry's framework also demands that managers be set up for future success—not just that they have outperformed historically, but that the conditions that allowed them to do so remain intact.

The Climate Strategy's Three-Bucket Structure

Most climate-oriented strategies concentrate in clean energy providers: solar, wind, and related infrastructure. Lombard Odier's approach, which underpins the Touchstone climate ETF, adds two additional sleeves that most competitors do not include. The transition leaders bucket captures carbon-intensive companies actively moving toward sustainability—a steel manufacturer on the path to decarbonization, for example, that might not qualify for most ESG screens but represents a genuine and measurable shift. The adaptation sleeve targets companies positioned to benefit from a physically warmer world: efficient air conditioning manufacturers are one example Barry cited explicitly. That third bucket reflects a candid recognition that some degree of warming is already priced into the physical environment, and that companies helping people and businesses adapt to that reality represent a real investment opportunity regardless of where emission reduction targets ultimately land.

Lombard Odier has been running this strategy in Europe, where it has raised over a billion dollars. The firm is several hundred billion dollars in assets under management and traces its history to the 1700s. It partners with Oxford for academic research input. The global portfolio runs approximately 40 to 50 high-conviction names, weighted by investment conviction and risk management analysis rather than market capitalization or equal weighting. Roughly 60 percent of the portfolio is in US-listed companies, with the remaining 40 percent international. In the ETF vehicle, the portfolio is rebalanced on a monthly basis, with custom basket mechanics used to manage tax efficiency through that process.

Securitized Income: Relative Value Across Four Building Blocks

Fort Washington's short-duration team built its expertise in securitized products after the financial crisis, when residential mortgage-backed securities offered compelling relative value and the team developed the surveillance tools needed to evaluate them rigorously. Over the past decade, the portfolio has expanded across all four major securitized categories—residential MBS, commercial MBS, asset-backed securities, and CLOs—with a strategic guideline of roughly 10 to 40 percent in each bucket. The team runs daily delinquency reports and cash flow analysis on underlying collateral, stress-tests portfolios against recessionary scenarios, and makes relative value decisions based on bottom-up assessment of individual issues rather than top-down factor tilts.

Notable Insights

"When you take a look at the universe of active managers in particular, you'll see a lot of strategies that have a few ailments that make it hard to generate Alpha—managers that are so big have tens or hundreds of billions of dollars and are by definition forced to look a lot like the benchmark."

"The thing that makes our climate ETF really unique is they've got a couple of other types of companies they're targeting as well—transition leaders, and companies that are going to benefit from the adaptation to a warmer environment."

"One of the key things about securitized income is it is really critical just to understand the underlying assets and what that collateral looks like—being able to stress test it, see if it's a recessionary scenario, what do we think the cash flow profile will look like."

Key Takeaways

  • Touchstone is a fully sub-advised $25 billion mutual fund manager that launched its ETF platform roughly 18 months before this conversation, applying the same SPIDR manager-selection discipline across both vehicles
  • The SPIDR framework—stability, personnel, infrastructure, discipline, results—prioritizes organizational durability and capacity constraints alongside performance track record
  • Lombard Odier's climate strategy is structured around three distinct buckets: clean energy solutions providers, transition leaders from carbon-intensive industries, and adaptation companies benefiting from a warmer physical environment—a construction that goes well beyond most ESG competitors
  • Fort Washington has managed the underlying securitized income strategy in mutual fund form for over a decade with a team together for more than 20 years; the ETF vehicle delivers the same process with enhanced tax efficiency via custom basket mechanics
  • The SEC's Rule 6c-11, adopted in 2019, was a structural catalyst for the active ETF expansion: it eliminated individual exemptive relief filings and made custom basket tools widely available to active managers for the first time
  • Both ETFs rebalance monthly in the ETF vehicle, driven by bottom-up relative value analysis and conviction weighting rather than fixed formulas

What This Means for Advisors

Touchstone's model—institutional sub-advisors, high-conviction mandates, and competitive landscape analysis before every launch—reflects how a mature mutual fund operation thinks about product quality in an ETF wrapper. The climate ETF's three-bucket structure is worth understanding carefully: advisors who have avoided climate thematics because of narrow clean-energy exposure may find Lombard Odier's approach meaningfully broader. The securitized income strategy, meanwhile, carries a historical risk-return profile that Barry argues compares favorably to high-yield corporate bonds at lower volatility and minimal interest rate duration—a combination worth consideration for advisors trying to add yield to fixed income sleeves without proportionately increasing rate sensitivity.

Listen to the full conversation with Matt Barry on Behind the Ticker, available wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Andrew Chanin on ProcureAM: Building from the Floor Up, Space ETFs, and Owning Your Infrastructure
metaDescription: Andrew Chanin of ProcureAM on building his own ETF infrastructure, the space investment thesis, and a first-to-market thematic approach.
content:
Andrew Chanin's path from the trading floor to founding Procure Asset Management is a story about the costs of trusting the wrong partners—and the conviction to build something better. On this episode of Behind the Ticker, Chanin walked Brad Roth through his career arc, the legal battle that shaped his views on ETF infrastructure, and how he identified space exploration as a defining investment theme before most investors were paying attention. His second product, a disaster restoration fund, rounds out a portfolio built around essential and often overlooked industries.

About Andrew Chanin and Procure Asset Management

Chanin's entry into the ETF industry was not planned. He showed up uninvited to an interview at a company called Kella Group on Broadway in lower Manhattan and left with the job. Kella Group operated the largest ETF specialist trading unit on the floor of the American Stock Exchange, and working there gave Chanin a ground-level education in how ETFs actually function. He worked his way up from floor clerk to lead market maker for international and global equity ETFs on NYSE Arca, then moved to a prop trading firm looking to build out its own ETF capabilities.

That work opened doors across the issuer and service provider landscape, and eventually led to his involvement in creating PureShares—a firm that brought 10 first-to-market ETFs to market, including what Chanin described as the world's first cybersecurity ETF. The next chapter came through a partnership with Robert Tull, a veteran widely credited with helping build the predecessor to iShares and with launching ETFs in numerous countries around the world while working with regulators and exchanges to open markets. Together, they founded Procure Holdings, with Procure AM as the registered investment advisor through which ETFs are issued. A separate trust structure delineates proprietary Procure products from any future partnership products, a distinction Chanin considers important for investor confidence.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Procure's inaugural product was designed to be first to market in a theme Chanin believed was still in its early innings: the global commercial space industry. The fund tracks an index developed in partnership with space policy and industry experts, including M. Walter Range—a former director at the Space Foundation who developed the most widely referenced model for estimating the size and growth of the global space market, still used by the nonprofit today.

The index methodology requires that, at each rebalance, at least 80 percent of the portfolio be comprised of companies deriving the majority of their revenues from space-related activities. The remaining allocation—up to 20 percent—can include more diversified aerospace and defense names that generate significant but not majority space revenues. All holdings must meet minimum market capitalization and daily liquidity thresholds. That structure produces a portfolio spanning launch companies, satellite manufacturers and operators, and communications and connectivity providers. The fund's second Procure-branded product, a disaster restoration strategy, extends this first-to-market philosophy into a different category of essential infrastructure.

Deeper Dive

Why Infrastructure Independence Mattered

Chanin's decision to build Procure's own trust and operational infrastructure was not philosophical—it was the direct result of watching products he had helped create get stripped away. He described a federal ruling in the Southern District of New York that found the products from his prior white-label experience had effectively been taken from him. That experience made the additional cost and complexity of owning the full infrastructure seem like a straightforward trade. With Tull's experience building ETF frameworks across regulatory environments in multiple countries, the team had the expertise to execute. Chanin was candid that independent infrastructure is not for everyone—it takes longer, costs more, and demands deep knowledge—but argued it makes sense for anyone who sees their business as a long-term enterprise rather than a single-product experiment.

The Geopolitical Dimension of the Space Market

The investment case for a space-focused portfolio is increasingly tied to geopolitics, not just commercial innovation. Chanin noted that early dominance of government funding—which once accounted for the vast majority of space industry spending—has shifted, with commercial activity now driving roughly 80 percent of total activity and government representing approximately 20 percent. But governments are now stepping back in. The conflict in Ukraine highlighted the strategic vulnerability of satellite dependence: Russia's ability to threaten British satellites at the Baikonur Cosmodrome early in the war was a visible signal that nations relying on third-party space infrastructure face meaningful risk.

China, meanwhile, has publicly identified what it calls the cislunar economy—the commercial and strategic space between Earth and the Moon—as a potential multi-trillion dollar opportunity over the coming decades. That framing is driving accelerating national investment in space infrastructure across allies and adversaries alike. Chanin sees this geopolitical ramp as an additional tailwind on top of the commercial cost reduction story.

Cost Reduction as the Central Enabling Innovation

When launch costs ran hundreds of thousands to millions of dollars per kilogram, space was functionally accessible only to governments and major defense contractors. Chanin's argument is that reusable rocket technology—driving those costs toward the hundreds or low thousands of dollars range—is the innovation that transforms the industry's total addressable market. More affordable launch opens the door to a broader range of commercial customers, which in turn supports more launch volume, which drives costs lower still. Chanin was careful to note that SpaceX, the most prominent driver of this shift, remains private, but argued that the cost dynamics it has introduced are already reshaping the competitive landscape for the publicly traded companies that populate the fund's index.

Notable Insights

"There is a lengthy federal ruling in the southern district of New York related to my experience as a white label client, and unfortunately the judge ruled that essentially our products were stolen from us."

"Before when it cost hundreds of thousands to millions of dollars to send a kilogram worth of material to space, it's not that affordable for many people to utilize it, but if you can get launch costs down to the hundreds to thousands of dollars range it truly opens up the market."

"The Moon is extremely strategic and they call this opportunity the cislunar economy—that's something that you look at what China is saying, they see this potentially as a multi-trillion dollar industry over the next couple of decades."

Key Takeaways

  • Procure was founded specifically to give Chanin full ownership of products and infrastructure, following a federal court ruling that products he created under a white-label arrangement had been taken from him
  • The space index requires at least 80 percent of holdings to derive majority revenues from space at each rebalance; up to 20 percent can be diversified aerospace and defense names with significant but non-majority space revenues
  • Index co-creator M. Walter Range is a former director of the Space Foundation who developed the most widely used model for sizing the global space industry, still applied by the nonprofit today
  • Reducing launch costs is the central enabling innovation: reusable rocket technology is expanding the addressable space market from governments and major contractors to a far broader set of commercial users
  • Geopolitical competition is returning government investment to the space sector even as the commercial share of total activity has grown substantially, creating dual tailwinds for the industry
  • Chanin's approach to thematic ETF construction follows a first-to-market philosophy, targeting sectors that have matured enough to be investable but early enough that the competitive landscape remains open

What This Means for Advisors

For advisors thinking about thematic allocations, the space industry presents a combination of long-duration growth drivers and near-term geopolitical tailwinds that is relatively uncommon in a single sector. The investment case is not primarily about moon tourism or Mars missions—it is about the infrastructure behind satellite communications, GPS, defense applications, and commercial launch services that underpins a growing share of daily economic activity. Chanin's index methodology, with its 80 percent pure-play threshold and inclusion of diversified names up to 20 percent, produces a portfolio broad enough to reduce single-company risk while maintaining genuine thematic focus. The second fund, built around disaster restoration, extends the same philosophy into a category where government spending and climate-driven demand may create a similar long-duration opportunity.

Listen to the full conversation with Andrew Chanin on Behind the Ticker, available wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Yuri Khodjamirian on Tema ETFs: Why Monopolies Are the Most Durable Investments You Can Own
metaDescription: Yuri Khodjamirian of Tema ETFs explains the five sources of monopoly advantage, the ROIC durability thesis, and the investment process behind the TOLL portfo...
content:
Yuri Khodjamirian started his investing career in July 2009—the bottom of the financial crisis—at a London boutique called Maedi Asset Management, where he spent roughly 15 years learning fundamental equity analysis before eventually co-running a UK income fund that peaked at around $2 billion. He joined Tema ETFs to help build what he describes as a new standard for active thematic investing, and his conversation with Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker covers how Tema defines monopolies with precision, why the best ones are actually good for their customers, and the four-pillar process the firm uses to build a concentrated conviction portfolio from a universe that can't be found with a screen.

About Yuri Khodjamirian and Tema ETFs

Khodjamirian trained at Maedi Asset Management, a boutique that spun out of a larger asset manager in 2002 and focused on long-term fundamental equity investing. He spent the bulk of his career there—starting as an analyst on a long/short global equity fund and later co-managing a UK income fund for six to seven years. When he left Maedi, he connected with Tema's founder Moritz and joined the firm roughly a year before this conversation, at which point Tema had launched five funds. The firm is explicitly focused on pioneering the active ETF space through what they call pure thematic universes: funds designed to express a single structural theme with clarity and precision rather than using a broad sector label as a proxy.

Khodjamirian manages the monopolies and oligopolies ETF, an actively managed product focused on companies whose competitive position is structural enough that he describes it, in many cases, as contractual. The name of the fund—and the ticker—reflects the toll-road metaphor he returns to repeatedly: businesses that sit at choke points, take a small cut of enormous transaction volumes, and do so for decades under terms that are often written into concession agreements.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Tema's monopolies thesis begins with a harder definition of competitive advantage than most moat-investing frameworks use. Khodjamirian identifies five distinct categories of genuine monopoly. The first is non-replicable physical assets—railroads are his example: there are six Class I railroads in the United States today, down from roughly 180 in the 1920s, and no new railroad will ever be built. The permits and capital required are prohibitive. The second is network effects, where each new user creates value for every existing user—financial exchanges, where liquidity begets more liquidity, are the clearest example. Third is high switching costs, as in medical devices, where changing vendors is so expensive in time and retraining that it almost never happens. Fourth is regulation, covering airports and toll roads where concessions often run 70 to 90 years—a horizon long enough that competitive dynamics are essentially frozen. Fifth is economies of scale, where a company has reached a cost structure that competitors cannot match; he cites semiconductor manufacturing as the extreme case, where the scale required to produce transistors competitively is, in his words, almost incomprehensible. What he explicitly excludes from the definition: intangible value. In his view, that's too weak a reason for competitive advantage—it can be broken far more easily than most investors assume.

The framework also addresses whether monopolies are actually good for consumers. His answer: the ones worth owning got there by building mission-critical products that generated enormous customer value over time. Visa, which together with one other firm controls roughly 84% of global network payments, is his example: not because the market share is modest, but because the product created real value for consumers and merchants alike, and Visa takes only a small toll on each transaction in return. The businesses that have done this—created value for customers, not extracted it—are the ones whose competitive positions have proven durable over decades.

Deeper Look: Insights from the Full Conversation

How Tema Builds the Portfolio Universe

The process starts with constructing what Tema calls a pure thematic universe—a hand-curated list of companies that genuinely express the monopolies theme, not a quantitative screen. Market share is one of the hardest numbers to find for any company, and even when available, it doesn't tell you whether the competitive position is structural or contestable. That judgment requires an analyst who has spent serious time mapping competitive dynamics industry by industry. Once the universe is established, Tema applies four pillars of investment analysis: a strong operating base, solid balance sheets and cash flow generation, a credible valuation case, and a clearly articulated investment edge. That last requirement is one Khodjamirian says fund managers often skip. Tema forces every analyst to answer it. A company has to clear all four tests before it enters the portfolio. The resulting portfolio targets approximately 30 to 35 holdings, with a floor of 25 that the team won't go below and a ceiling the team won't exceed in the near term.

Return on Invested Capital and the Durability Problem

Khodjamirian's central investment insight is that return on invested capital is well-understood as a quality metric, but the market systematically underprices its durability in monopolistic businesses. Standard financial analysis assumes returns will eventually fade back toward the cost of capital—and for most companies, that assumption is correct. For a business holding a 90-year airport concession, or a railroad that physically cannot be replicated, that fade never materializes. "The market just cannot price this properly," he said, "and that's why you see share prices of these companies go up over time—they're constantly beating expectations of the fade." The implication is precise: buying high-ROIC companies is not sufficient. The question is whether that ROIC is durable, and most of Tema's analytical work is devoted to stress-testing durability rather than identifying ROIC in the first place.

Why the Best Monopolies Keep Reinvesting

Khodjamirian pushed back on the assumption that monopolies coast on their competitive position. The companies that belong in the portfolio, in his view, are actively reinvesting in their advantages rather than extracting maximum rent from them. He used Moody's as a specific example: the company holds what he described as the largest database of private and public companies in the world—a proprietary data asset that becomes more valuable as AI capabilities improve. "People think about Nvidia when they think about AI, but they should be thinking about companies like Moody's that have enormous proprietary databases, beautiful for artificial intelligence to be used as a feature and a layer. And Moody's is investing heavily in that." The firms that do this—reinvest their structural advantages into the next durable edge—are the ones that compound over decades without the fade.

Notable Insights

"The reason why monopoly is such a good investment is because of the durability of that return and that quality. All our time is spent understanding that durability."

"It's not enough to just buy high-ROIC companies. What you need is ROIC companies that have durability over time."

"The market is very conditioned on returns to come back to the cost of capital. For these companies, this doesn't really happen. And that's why you see share prices go up over time—they're constantly beating expectations of the fade."

Key Takeaways

  • Tema defines monopolies using five specific structural sources: non-replicable physical assets, network effects, high switching costs, regulation, and economies of scale—intangible value is explicitly excluded as too fragile
  • The best monopolies built their competitive position by creating genuine customer value over time, not by extraction—that distinction is central to assessing long-term durability
  • Portfolio construction starts with a hand-curated thematic universe, not a screen, because the relevant signals require analyst judgment about competitive dynamics rather than data retrieval
  • Tema's four-pillar process requires strong operating base, solid balance sheet and cash flow, a valuation case, and a clearly articulated investment edge—all four must be present
  • Return on invested capital is widely understood; what the market consistently underprices is its durability, and Tema's research is focused on that underpriced variable
  • The portfolio targets 30 to 35 holdings, always above 25, with concentrated conviction rather than diversification as a structural goal

What This Means for Advisers

The framework Khodjamirian uses answers a question that often doesn't get asked directly in thematic investing: what makes this theme durable, not just interesting? The difference between a theme and a structural advantage is the difference between a narrative and a contractual reality. For advisers evaluating long-term equity exposure, Tema's monopolies portfolio offers something specific—not a bet on a sector or macro story, but a systematic effort to identify businesses where competition has been effectively neutralized by physical, regulatory, or network realities that won't change on a five-year horizon. The ROIC durability framing is also a useful analytical lens for evaluating any quality-oriented allocation, not just the companies in this particular portfolio. If your due diligence on a quality holding doesn't include an honest assessment of how long the returns can persist before fading, the valuation work that follows may be built on an overly optimistic foundation.

The full conversation with Yuri Khodjamirian is available on Behind the Ticker. Subscribe wherever you listen to podcasts.

title: Ragen Stienke on Ballast Asset Management: The Case for Quantifying Downside First
metaDescription: Ragen Stienke of Ballast Asset Management explains the downside-first framework, management team evaluation, and the small/midcap opportunity behind MGMT.
content:
Ragen Stienke didn't start his career as a portfolio manager. He started it valuing businesses at Arthur Anderson—learning, as he put it, to value things in the real world as opposed to an academic perspective. That foundation shapes everything about how Ballast Asset Management approaches investing, from how they evaluate management teams to how they size positions. Stienke joined Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker to walk through the investment process behind their small and midcap active ETF, with particular focus on downside quantification, management team assessment, and why behavioral finance isn't just an academic interest at Ballast—it's a hard operating constraint.

About Ragen Stienke and Ballast Asset Management

Stienke founded Ballast Asset Management in 2015, the day after leaving Westwood, where he and longtime business partner Jay Sing had launched the firm's midcap strategy, raised $3.5 billion, and built a sister strategy that attracted another billion in assets. He has worked with his two primary partners for 30-plus and 20-plus years respectively—Jay Sing from the Arthur Anderson days in the mid-1990s, Tom Fogarty from UBS in New York. Ballast runs a single strategy across SMAs and an active ETF, and Stienke describes that focus as a 20-year commitment: fundamental small and midcap investing with a systematic emphasis on asymmetric risk-reward.

The firm historically managed primarily institutional capital—roughly 85 to 90% of the $4.5 billion raised across both strategies was institutional. About three years before this conversation, following an SEC opinion letter that allowed active managers to operate with the same structural efficiency in an ETF as passive vehicles, Ballast launched what Stienke believes was the first small or small-and-midcap actively managed ETF of its kind. The ETF runs the identical strategy as the SMA, just in a different vehicle for accessing the same process.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Ballast's stated goal is to turn alpha generation into a manufacturing process rather than a process dependent on any individual being right. The cornerstone is quantifying downside before ever building an upside case. Stienke is explicit that this is not the same as buying at a discount to intrinsic value or at a margin of safety in the conventional sense. Ballast tries to buy within 20 to 30% of the worst case they can envision. Once they have that downside number, they build a three-year base case for the business, generate a price target, and calculate what they call a reward-to-risk ratio. They invest when that ratio reaches at least 3:1. The process is designed to be repeated consistently—not driven by conviction or probability assessments, both of which Stienke views as reintroducing the behavioral biases the framework is meant to eliminate. He cites Kahneman and Tversky as formative references for the firm's thinking, and acknowledges that while their work isn't required reading at Ballast, it's heavily encouraged.

The small and midcap space creates opportunity partly because it has been progressively under-covered. Decimalization in the early 2000s drove commission rates from five to seven cents down to a penny or less per share, and the large brokerage firms that previously used trading desk commissions to fund sell-side analyst coverage of smaller names largely stopped doing so. Over the past 20 years, Stienke has observed a meaningful drop in coverage for the businesses Ballast owns—creating informational advantages for investors willing to do primary research themselves.

Deeper Look: Insights from the Full Conversation

How Ballast Quantifies Downside

The approach varies by business type, but the discipline is consistent: what happens to this business under genuine stress? For a software company, Stienke looks at the recurring revenue base, applies realistic renewal rates, assumes new product growth goes to zero, and calculates the liquidation value of that maintenance stream—or references what comparable businesses have sold for in secondary markets, typically three to five times maintenance revenue. For a bank, the team builds a burndown analysis on the loan book category by category, referencing actual loss rates from prior recessions across mortgage, commercial real estate, and other segments. For a shipping company, they've valued the fleet in secondary markets under distressed conditions. The upside case can look exceptionally attractive, but if the downside is too large or too uncertain to quantify, Ballast won't invest.

The Management Checklist

Stienke has written a white paper on management evaluation. The framework has three components. First: alignment—and the question isn't how much management gets paid, but what metrics determine their pay. A retail CEO compensated on revenue growth has every incentive to open more stores whether or not incremental capital is being deployed productively. Ballast digs into the compensation structure specifically to see whether management wins when their investors win. Second: track record of capital deployment across prior jobs and prior divisions—not just the current role. Third: fit—whether the management team is the right type for the company at its current stage. A growth-focused operator running a mature business that needs operational discipline is a mismatch; a purely operational executive in an early-stage company is another. When those misalignments exist and then shift—a new CEO, a compensation structure changed to include a return on capital component—that transition can be a buy catalyst for Ballast to initiate or build a position.

Position Sizing and Portfolio Architecture

Ballast doesn't equal-weight. Position sizing is driven by the reward-to-risk ratio: the greater the asymmetry between upside and downside, the larger the position, up to a 3% maximum at purchase. As a stock appreciates and the ratio compresses, the position gets trimmed systematically. Layered on top is a proprietary risk management model that aggregates fundamental exposures—revenue and earnings broken down by cyclicality, market sensitivity, and other factors—across all holdings, giving the portfolio management team a holistic view of what the portfolio actually looks like at the aggregate level. Stienke describes it this way: an analyst's job is to paint perfect brush strokes on a canvas; the portfolio manager's job is to step back and confirm the picture makes sense. There is no set rebalance schedule—rebalancing occurs purely when positions are being bought or sold on a stock-by-stock basis.

Notable Insights

"What we are trying to accomplish is to turn alpha generation into a manufacturing process rather than a process that is reliant on either my or any of my teammates' ability to be right all the time. That really starts with a focus on quantifying downside."

"Having worked at Arthur Anderson early in my career, I can tell you that companies are able to manufacture earnings through accounting gimmickry. The one thing you can't distort is cash going into and out of a bank account."

"We are the firm that looks both ways when we cross a one-way street."

Key Takeaways

  • Ballast runs a single small and midcap strategy applied identically across SMAs and an active ETF, with 20 years of track record across both institutional and wealth channels
  • The core framework requires a reward-to-risk ratio of at least 3:1, built by quantifying downside to the worst plausible scenario before ever modeling the upside case
  • Management analysis is systematic across three dimensions: compensation alignment, capital deployment track record, and stage fit—a change in management or compensation structure can be a buy catalyst
  • Downside quantification methods vary by business type: recurring revenue burndowns for software, loan book stress tests by category for banks, distressed fleet valuations for shipping companies
  • Position sizing is driven by the reward-to-risk ratio with a 3% cap at purchase, overlaid with a fundamentally driven portfolio risk model that aggregates exposures holistically
  • The small and midcap informational opportunity has arguably widened over 20 years as sell-side coverage has declined—rewarding investors willing to do primary research

What This Means for Advisers

Ballast offers something distinct from the quantitative and thematic products that dominate the active ETF landscape: a fundamentally driven, behaviorally disciplined process that starts with the question most managers skip—what happens if I'm wrong? For advisers evaluating active small-and-midcap exposure, the management assessment framework and the downside-first construction are worth understanding in detail before making an allocation. The process was designed explicitly to eliminate conviction-based decision-making, which means the discipline holds even when individual holdings are under pressure—a meaningful attribute in a market segment where volatility is higher and analyst coverage is thinner. Stienke's point about the informational edge available in less-covered companies also matters: a team that has been exploiting it systematically for 20 years across $4.5 billion in institutional capital brings a different baseline than most ETF alternatives in the category.

The full episode with Ragen Stienke is available on Behind the Ticker. Subscribe wherever you listen to podcasts.

title: Steve Cook on Harbor Funds: Better Commodity Exposure and Why the NYSE Floor Changes the Game for Advisers
metaDescription: Steve Cook of Harbor Capital explains the HEDGER commodity ETF, NYSE floor listing benefits, and Harbor's manager-of-managers approach to ETFs.
content:
Steve Cook has been in the ETF business long enough to remember when there was no centralized data repository and floor traders called his desk for basket updates on the Nasdaq 100 ETF. As head of ETFs at Harbor Capital Advisors, he joined Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker to trace Harbor's evolution from pension manager-of-managers to ETF issuer, explain why moving ETFs to the NYSE floor materially improves adviser outcomes, and make the case for a commodity ETF built from the ground up with inflation protection as the actual objective—not a repurposed corporate hedging index.

About Steve Cook and Harbor Capital Advisors

Harbor Capital Advisors traces its roots to the pension management arm of Libby Glass and several Ohio manufacturing companies, founded roughly 40 years ago on a single core capability: finding exceptional investment managers in niche asset classes and packaging them for institutional investors. The executive team believed their manager selection was strong enough to build a stand-alone business, and they spun out into a professional mutual fund firm. The model worked. Harbor was the first to offer access to Pimco in a retail mutual fund structure, and the firm has maintained a 50-plus-year relationship with Janus and Associates, whose capital appreciation strategy remains Harbor's largest mutual fund today.

After launching collective investment trusts about four years ago for the retirement market, Harbor entered the ETF space in September 2021—applying the same manager-sourcing philosophy to the wealth channel. Cook's own background began at the very start of the modern ETF era. When Nasdaq launched the Nasdaq 100 ETF, Bank of New York was selected as trustee. Early ETFs were structured as unit investment trusts to avoid the compliance burden of a fully registered investment company, and the trustee carried substantial operating responsibility. Cook got direct exposure to every layer of the ETF ecosystem—index management, capital markets, trading—because the infrastructure that would later become standard simply didn't exist yet.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Harbor's approach to bringing new products to market follows a consistent framework. The investment research team evaluates potential managers on two criteria: Is the alpha repeatable, or does it reflect a factor tilt or benchmark-hugging? And is there a genuine commitment to continuously improving the underlying process? Once a manager clears due diligence, Harbor works with them over roughly three to six months to adapt their process for the ETF structure—adjusting trading approach, rebalance frequency, or number of holdings to improve tradability—before finalizing service provider relationships and market maker partnerships.

The HEDGER product came through a relationship Harbor's president had with the team at Quantic Commodities. The insight behind the fund starts with a structural gap: the widely used commodity indices—BCOM and GSCI—were built to help manufacturers and corporate users hedge commodity exposure in their businesses, not to protect investor portfolios from inflation. Quantic built the Total Return Commodity Index from the ground up to solve that problem. The team brings serious practitioner experience: Don Casturo ran Goldman Sachs' commodity trading desk for more than 20 years, and the other principals were managing directors or partners at Goldman in commodities. The index can access up to 24 commodities across energy (Brent, WTI, heating oil, gas oil), grains (corn, wheat, soybeans), softs (cotton, sugar), base metals (copper, aluminum, nickel, zinc), and precious metals. Gold currently carries roughly a 30% allocation, with exposure adjusted quarterly based on debasement and scarcity indicators. The overall commodity mix reconstitutes annually based on economic weight and roll yield optimization. Cook describes the product as rules-based passive in structure, but with enough dynamism in the index construction to deliver what he calls quasi-active management within the passive wrapper.

Deeper Look: Insights from the Full Conversation

The NYSE Floor: What It Actually Solves

Cook made the case for NYSE floor listing with enough precision that the business logic becomes hard to argue with. The problem is mechanical: when a fund holds 150 names, every one of those securities goes through independent price discovery as the market opens. A three-cent swing in each underlying name compounds into spreads at the ETF level that can look far wider than the portfolio's fair value. The complication is that advisers using platform-based workflows often default to market-on-open orders for block trades across client accounts—the exact orders most exposed to opening-auction volatility. A floor specialist inserts a human into the transaction who can look at the context of an incoming order and make a fair market accordingly. Cook reported that since moving products to the floor, no market-on-open trade has come in more than three cents above fair value. Brad confirmed from his own experience: prior to the move, 30,000-share orders were executing ten cents wide from where they should have been.

Manager Sourcing and the Due Diligence Filter

Harbor's sourcing is bidirectional. Some managers approach Harbor with ideas; others are identified by the internal research team. Either way, the due diligence process is identical. The key question isn't whether a manager produced returns historically—it's whether the source of those returns is durable and repeatable. The IR team specifically looks for evidence of ongoing process improvement, not just historical performance. That distinction—between alpha that compounds because the process keeps getting better versus alpha that was a factor tilt that happened to work—is central to how Harbor evaluates every new manager relationship.

Portfolio Fit for Commodities

When working with advisers, Cook positions HEDGER as a solution to structural underweighting in commodities. The conversation isn't about tactical positioning; it's about what's absent from most model portfolios from a protection standpoint. His view is that investors have chronically underallocated to commodities, and that HEDGER offers a purpose-built tool for the actual use case—an inflation hedge built around the economic cycle—as opposed to a generic index repurposed from corporate hedging applications.

Notable Insights

"The constant preaching is: you want to avoid the first 15 minutes of trading in an ETF, you want to avoid the last 15 minutes of trading in an ETF. And throughout the course of the day, for more thinly traded ETFs, as best you can, if you're not using a block order, you want to use limit orders to avoid these wide spreads occurring in the product."

"Everything that's out there wasn't purpose-built for the reason it's being utilized. And we think there's a better solution that we can create for investors to give them exposure to commodities during periods of inflation that will protect a portfolio."

"We have to meet advisers where they are. If they put in at 9:31 a market-on-open order or at 3:59 a market-on-close order, they're going to get good execution. By moving down to the floor, it really has done that."

Key Takeaways

  • Harbor spent 35 years as a manager-of-managers before entering the ETF space in September 2021, applying the same sourcing and due diligence philosophy to the wealth channel
  • The HEDGER product uses the Quantic Total Return Commodity Index, built specifically for inflation protection—not corporate hedging—by former Goldman Sachs commodity practitioners with decades of trading desk experience
  • The index accesses up to 24 commodities, reconstitutes annually, and rebalances quarterly with dynamic gold weighting adjusted by debasement and scarcity indicators
  • NYSE floor listing resolves the structural conflict between automated opening-auction volatility and market-on-open advisory orders, with measurable improvement in execution quality
  • Harbor's due diligence framework screens for repeatable, process-driven alpha—not factor tilt—and requires evidence of continuous improvement from every manager partner
  • Cook views structural commodity underweighting in model portfolios as the core problem HEDGER addresses, positioning it as a strategic inflation hedge rather than a tactical trade

What This Means for Advisers

The floor listing point has direct practical implications. If you're placing block trades across accounts on a market-on-open basis—which many platform workflows default to—you may be absorbing execution slippage that compounds across clients and positions. Cook's data point from Harbor's experience and Brad's from THOR's own ETF both indicate the improvement is real and measurable. On the product side, HEDGER offers what most commodity ETFs don't: an index whose construction was actually designed for the use case advisers have in mind—protecting client portfolios during inflationary periods—built by people who ran commodity desks professionally for over two decades. Understanding the index's construction logic, particularly how gold weighting adjusts based on debasement and scarcity signals, is worth a closer look before making an allocation decision.

The full conversation with Steve Cook is available now on Behind the Ticker. Subscribe wherever you listen to podcasts.

title: Katie Stockton on Technical Analysis, Sector Rotation, and Risk-First Investing
metaDescription: Fairlead Strategies founder Katie Stockton on her rules-based sector rotation ETF, technical analysis as a risk tool, and how the model navigated 2022.
content:
Katie Stockton came to technical analysis through an unlikely entry point—a hand-charting internship at Dorsey Wright & Associates, a Richmond firm she discovered when she spotted a pile of point-and-figure charts on an advisor's desk during a college internship. That early exposure shaped every professional decision she made afterward, from the Morgan Stanley technical strategy team to E-Offering's technology stock research desk during the dot-com era to launching Fairlead Strategies in 2018. On this episode of Behind the Ticker, Brad sits down with Katie to discuss how Fairlead approaches markets, what her rules-based sector rotation ETF is actually designed to do, and why she believes technical analysis is one of the most effective risk management tools available to investors who are willing to follow the math rather than their instincts.

About Katie Stockton and Fairlead Strategies

Katie Stockton founded Fairlead Strategies in early 2018 after spending the majority of her career on the sell side at Wall Street firms. The name "Fairlead" comes from a nautical term—a part on a boat designed to allow lines to run free without getting tangled—a subtle nod to technical analysis as a clarifying discipline. Katie is also a CNBC contributor and has spent time at Morgan Stanley's technical strategy team (now no longer in existence), at E-Offering during the dot-com period, and most recently at BTI, an investment bank in Manhattan.

Her exposure to institutional clients across those roles gave her a direct window into what investors actually found useful. Point-and-figure charts, which she learned to hand-chart at Dorsey Wright, required starting from square one in any client presentation with a non-technical audience. Bar charts with clear indicators were more accessible and became her working discipline. Fairlead now publishes seven research products: a daily report oriented around short-term internals and S&P futures flow; three weekly reports including the comprehensive top-down "Fairlead Tactics" report covering the S&P 500, sectors, crude oil, gold, the dollar, and treasury yields; an ETF Navigator covering approximately 60 ETFs with apples-to-apples comparisons across asset classes; a Substack newsletter for more opportunistic and retail-oriented ideas; and cryptocurrency research.

Investment Strategy and Approach

Fairlead's sector rotation ETF, launched in March 2022, grew directly out of the research the firm was already producing. Katie noticed that many of her advisory clients were already doing informal sector rotation—moving in and out of sector SPDR ETFs based on where they saw momentum—but their approach wasn't systematic. The ETF was designed to make that process explicit, rules-based, and disciplined.

The model starts with eight equal-weighted buckets. In a confirmed bull market with established long-term upside momentum, all eight buckets are filled with sector ETFs. The three defensive sectors—real estate, utilities, and consumer staples—are excluded because they tend to underperform enough in strong markets that including them adds little value. The eight remaining buckets represent non-defensive equity sectors, evaluated using the State Street sector SPDR funds because of their liquidity, low cost, and familiarity among advisors.

Each sector is evaluated against three criteria: long-term momentum, long-term overbought and oversold conditions, and long-term relative strength versus the broader market. When a sector fails to qualify, its bucket doesn't sit in cash—it rotates into one of three alternative asset classes that have historically held up better than equities during market stress: long-term treasuries, short-term treasuries (one to three years), and gold. That diversified defensive allocation was especially important during 2022, when long-term bond funds fell alongside equities—a combination that damaged portfolios built around a simple equity-to-long-duration-treasury toggle.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

Reacting Is Not the Same as Predicting

A recurring theme throughout the conversation is the distinction Katie draws between reactive and predictive approaches to markets. Fairlead's methodology is explicitly reactive: the model follows what the market is doing—trend direction, momentum, relative strength—rather than trying to anticipate where it will go. Katie describes the goal as responding to "trends, trend shifts, momentum shifts, overbought oversold indications" with as little interpretation as possible. The discipline of maintaining a chartbook, she argues, is a direct check on bias: you cannot rationalize away what a chart's indicators are showing you, and you cannot argue against the trend. That structure is what allows the firm to apply technical analysis at scale, across dozens of ETFs, without letting conviction or emotion distort the output.

A Career Built on the Discipline

Katie's path into technical analysis began at the old PaineWebber office in Richmond, where she spotted point-and-figure charts on an advisor's desk and traced them back to Dorsey Wright & Associates—a nearby firm that hired her part-time for roughly two years during college. She learned to hand-chart, developed close familiarity with price data, and found a professional direction she has followed ever since. Her university was one of only twelve at the time to offer coursework in technical analysis, a graduate-level course she audited as an undergraduate with practitioners including Ralph Acampora. Later, in client-facing sell-side roles, she found that bar charts with clear indicators were far more accessible than point-and-figure for non-technical audiences—more a communication decision than a methodological one.

Ringing the Bell

The launch of Fairlead's ETF brought an opportunity Katie describes as the pinnacle of her career: ringing the opening bell at the New York Stock Exchange. She had been to the NYSE many times for CNBC broadcasts but never in that role. What made the moment wasn't the ceremony—it was looking around the room and seeing colleagues, friends, family, and her children there to celebrate the launch. "I don't think in the earlier stages of my career I could have ever imagined having that opportunity," she says. Launching the ETF wasn't part of the original plan when she founded Fairlead as an independent research firm; it emerged as the logical product expression of the research the firm was already doing and the demand advisors were already showing for a systematic, investable version of the sector rotation strategy.

Notable Insights

"You can't argue against the trend. You can't argue against the indicators. And to have that apples-to-apples comparison is a great way to go about that—a more conservative approach to technical analysis."

"We're not trying to be ultra predictive in our work. We're rather trying to react to trends, trend shifts, momentum shifts, overbought oversold indications, things of that nature—and we think that's one of the best ways to invest long-term in the equity market."

"I'm almost unemotional about the whole thing because I don't think we can even get too passionate about anything that's coming from math. It's just math."

Key Takeaways

  • Fairlead Strategies is an independent technical research firm founded in 2018, publishing seven research products across daily, weekly, and opportunistic cadences for institutional and advisory clients.
  • The sector rotation ETF uses eight equal-weighted buckets filled with non-defensive sector ETFs in bull markets, rotating into diversified alternative asset classes when sectors fail to qualify.
  • Sectors are evaluated on three criteria: long-term momentum, long-term overbought/oversold conditions, and relative strength versus the broader market.
  • When no qualifying sectors exist, the model rotates into a diversified combination of long-term treasuries, short-term treasuries, and gold—not cash—a structure that helped the fund navigate 2022's simultaneous equity and bond bear market.
  • Technical analysis, in Katie's framework, is primarily a risk management tool and a check against bias—not a prediction engine.
  • Fairlead's ETF Navigator covers approximately 60 ETFs with consistent, apples-to-apples technical comparisons updated on a weekly basis across rotating asset class coverage.

What This Means for Advisors

For advisors who already think in terms of sector rotation but implement it informally—making judgment calls about when to lean into energy or rotate out of technology—Fairlead's model offers a systematic alternative with a transparent, rules-based framework. The research underpinning the ETF is available directly to advisory clients as a standalone subscription, meaning advisors can engage with the methodology and test its logic before committing to a product allocation. The ETF Navigator in particular, covering 60 ETFs with weekly apples-to-apples chart comparisons, is designed to give advisors a consistent technical view across their holdings and help them avoid letting narrative or recency bias drive allocation decisions that the data doesn't support.

Listen

Katie Stockton's conversation with Brad covers the full arc of her career in technical analysis and the thinking behind one of the more rigorously constructed rules-based ETFs in the market today. Hear the full episode of Behind the Ticker wherever you listen to podcasts.

title: Will Rhind on GranitShares: Building High-Conviction ETFs for the Modern Investor
metaDescription: GranitShares CEO Will Rhind on single stock leverage ETFs, how stocks get selected, and the structural edge the US market holds over Europe.
content:
Will Rhind has been building ETFs for more than two decades—from the early days of iShares in Europe, to running the world's largest gold fund, to launching GranitShares in 2016. His firm has taken a deliberately different path in the ETF industry: not broad beta, not passive index replication, but high-conviction products designed for investors who have a specific view and want targeted exposure to it. On this episode of Behind the Ticker, Brad sits down with Will to break down how GranitShares builds single stock leverage ETFs, how the firm decides which underlying stocks to build products around, and why Will believes the US market holds structural advantages that Europe simply cannot replicate.

About Will Rhind and GranitShares

Will Rhind's ETF career began in the early 2000s in the UK, where he was part of the iShares team at Barclays Global Investors—now BlackRock—helping launch some of the first ETFs in Europe. He later co-founded a startup that was acquired by WisdomTree, then served as CEO of the World Gold Council's flagship gold ETF, the largest gold and commodity fund in the world, run in partnership with State Street. In 2016, he left to found GranitShares.

The firm's positioning is explicit: "high conviction ETFs for the high conviction investor." The original lineup included commodity and income strategies before GranitShares expanded into single stock leverage—products providing daily leveraged or inverse exposure to individual companies including Tesla, Coinbase, Nvidia, and AMD. The firm first developed and proved these products in Europe, where it already operated across major markets, before bringing them to the United States. As of the time of this episode, GranitShares managed approximately $1.7 billion in assets under management.

Investment Strategy and Approach

GranitShares' single stock leverage products work through the same mechanism as other leveraged ETFs: they gain exposure via swap contracts with investment banks, which provide the stated leverage factor to the fund in exchange for collateral and margin. The funds rebalance at the end of each trading day to maintain that leverage factor—which is why the products target a daily return rather than a long-term compounding result.

The amount of leverage GranitShares can deploy on any given stock is constrained by SEC derivatives rules tied directly to the underlying security's volatility. More volatile stocks carry lower leverage targets; less volatile ones can support higher multiples. This is why the Nvidia product carries a 1.5x exposure while others in the lineup differ, and why the lineup looks heterogeneous rather than uniform across products.

Will is candid about stock selection: "It's definitely more of an art than a science." The firm draws on its European experience—where it already has a broad single stock leverage book—to identify names that resonate with high-conviction retail investors globally. Tesla, for example, consistently ranks as a top-one or top-two holding in retail portfolios across virtually every country. Coinbase was chosen because it functioned as the only way to get leveraged crypto exposure inside an ETF wrapper at the time of launch. The Nvidia product caught the AI wave and became the firm's biggest success story of the year at the time of recording.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

The Volatility Rule That Shapes Every Product

The SEC derivatives rule isn't background regulation—it's the architectural constraint around which each single stock leverage product is designed. The rule ties maximum allowable leverage to the volatility of the underlying security. That means a high-volatility stock will carry a lower stated leverage factor than a more stable one, and issuers cannot simply apply a uniform multiplier across their lineup. This design forces product-specific calibration and is the direct reason GranitShares' products come in varied leverage factors. It also serves as a built-in risk governor: the products that attract the most speculative interest tend to be the ones where the derivatives rule most constrains how much leverage can be applied.

Marketing First, Sales Second

GranitShares deliberately built a marketing-led distribution approach rather than a traditional wholesaler model. Will describes their target audience as a global community of high-conviction investors who tend to use online and mobile trading platforms and generally arrive already knowing what they want to trade. Reaching them means digital marketing and earned PR rather than shelf-space negotiations with distributors or relationship-driven wholesaler calls. The firm uses marketing to create awareness and digital techniques to direct that audience toward the product. That calculus makes sense for a product set that draws retail and active trader demand rather than model portfolio inclusion by RIAs.

Why the US Market Is Structurally Different from Europe

Will spent years running European ETF operations before building GranitShares here, so his comparison carries firsthand weight. The structural advantage the US holds, in his framing, exists before any discussion of product strategy: one currency, one exchange, one regulator, and one language. Those four factors mean US liquidity pools aren't fragmented, scale is achievable without rebuilding distribution infrastructure country by country, and regulatory interaction is consolidated. In Europe, each of those decisions has to be remade in each market. Some observers say Europe's ETF market is roughly five years behind the US. Will believes the structural advantages the US holds won't disappear in his lifetime.

Notable Insights

"The barrier to entry in ETFs has never been as low as it is today. The barrier to success, however, has never been higher—and that's because the product proliferation means it's just harder and harder to get eyeballs on a new launch."

"The way the word we use internally is we say the platform is alive, meaning that just on a daily basis we see tons of activity. We have creates, redeems every day. Orders coming through."

"The US has an amazing advantage before you start talking about any intricacies, because you have one currency, you have one exchange, you have one regulator, and you have one language."

Key Takeaways

  • GranitShares is built around "high conviction ETFs for the high conviction investor"—deliberately avoiding the generic benchmark products that dominate industry flows.
  • Single stock leverage ETFs gain their exposure through daily swap contracts with investment banks; they target a stated leverage factor each day and reset at close.
  • SEC derivatives rules tie maximum leverage to underlying stock volatility, which is why leverage factors vary across the product lineup rather than being uniform.
  • Stock selection draws on European market experience and is more art than science—universal retail appeal, first-mover status in a niche, and proxy use cases all factor into the decision.
  • GranitShares leads with marketing and PR to reach online, mobile-first investors rather than building a traditional wholesaler sales model.
