  • The US ETF market holds four structural advantages Europe cannot easily replicate: one currency, one exchange, one regulator, and one language—each of which compounds into meaningful liquidity and scale benefits.

What This Means for Advisors

Single stock leverage products are not core portfolio holdings, and Will doesn't frame them that way. Their natural home is with the active, high-conviction investor or advisor who believes their job is to generate alpha rather than simply track a benchmark—technical traders, stock pickers, managers with concentrated directional views. For those advisors, daily-reset leverage products inside an ETF wrapper offer a way to express those views without the margin mechanics of futures or the complexity of options. The daily reset and volatility drag in trending markets are genuine structural considerations, but for advisors already trading individual stocks actively, the product logic is more intuitive than it might initially appear.

Listen

Will Rhind's conversation with Brad is one of the more candid discussions of ETF product development, market structure, and international strategy to appear on Behind the Ticker. Hear the full episode wherever you listen to podcasts.

title: Paul Weisbruch on Market Making: The Infrastructure Behind Every ETF Launch
metaDescription: GTS's Paul Weisbruch on what market makers do, how ETF launches go wrong, and why capital markets teams matter from day one.
content:
Paul Weisbruch has been inside the ETF industry longer than most people have been paying attention to it. From a specialist post at the Philadelphia Stock Exchange handling the country's earliest equity index ETFs, to a stint on the issuer side at Pacer, to his current role at GTS—one of the most active lead market makers on the New York Stock Exchange—Paul's career traces the full arc of the ETF industry's maturation. On this episode of Behind the Ticker, Brad sits down with Paul to explore what market makers actually do, what separates a clean ETF launch from a chaotic one, and why having a capital markets team in place before day one matters more than most issuers realize.

About Paul Weisbruch and GTS

Paul Weisbruch has been active in the ETF ecosystem since the early 2000s, beginning on the NASDAQ OTC market making desk at Susquehanna before moving to the Philadelphia Stock Exchange to work the specialist post for two of the earliest US-listed equity index ETFs. At the time, he recalls, there were only a handful of ETFs in existence, spreads were wide, and volumes were comparatively modest. No one was talking about thousands of funds, mutual fund conversions, or major asset managers entering the space at scale.

He later spent time at Pacer ETFs in 2008 and 2009 on the issuer side—a period that gave him direct exposure to the distribution network and the criteria advisors and institutions use when screening and adding ETFs to their portfolios. Paul joined GTS in 2019 following GTS's acquisition of a market making group, and he now leads market making at the firm. GTS currently serves as lead market maker for 341 US-listed ETFs and secondary market maker for an additional 40, with trading activity across more than 3,200 US-listed exchange-traded products.

How GTS Approaches Market Making

GTS's edge, according to Paul, sits at the intersection of technology and institutional-grade market experience. The firm's auto-quoting infrastructure posts continuous two-sided quotes across hundreds of names from market open to close every day—no gaps, no breaks. But the technology alone isn't what differentiates the firm. What separates GTS is its ability to recognize the distinct needs of different client types: a broker-dealer block desk trading for thousands of advisors at once has entirely different priorities than an RIA running an ETF model portfolio, which in turn trades differently than a large institutional plan making occasional but very large ETF purchases.

Paul describes GTS's role as frequently consultative—examining a client's current ETF holdings, flagging product nuances they may not have considered, and helping them understand how a fund might behave when traded at meaningful size. Many GTS team members have ETF wholesaling backgrounds, which means they understand not just how the product trades but how it gets distributed and what advisors actually weigh when evaluating a new fund.

One structural advantage Paul points to is GTS's Designated Market Maker model on the New York Stock Exchange, which places a human layer on top of the firm's algorithmic quoting systems. That combination tends to produce tighter spreads, greater market depth, and better optics for advisors evaluating a new fund for the first time. As Paul puts it, when a fund shows tight spreads and visible market activity, the barrier for an advisor to make a sizable allocation drops considerably.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

What Floor Traders Actually Do

The NYSE floor trader role is widely misunderstood as ceremonial. Paul corrects that directly. The specialist's core obligation is to provide two-sided quotes and maintain an orderly market at all times—including when order flow is entirely one-directional. In periods of genuine market stress, when there may be nothing but sellers or nothing but buyers, the floor specialist is often the only source of liquidity. They must keep quoting, absorb positions into their own book when necessary, and match buyers and sellers where possible. They also develop a detailed awareness of which firms are active at which price levels on any given day—information that helps them navigate large, complex order flows without creating unnecessary price impact.

What Has to Happen Before an ETF Launches

The most common pre-launch oversight, in Paul's experience, is assuming that distribution channels are automatically ready to trade a new fund on day one. They often are not. Paul strongly recommends that issuers contact the custodians their clients use—firms like Schwab, Fidelity, LPL, and Raymond James—well before launch to confirm the new symbol and CUSIP are active in each firm's master database and can accept orders. He has seen situations where an advisor tried to place an order on launch day and received a rejection simply because the symbol hadn't been added. That represents lost business for a problem that's entirely preventable with a few phone calls made in advance.

Why a Capital Markets Function Changes Everything

Paul's second major recommendation is to have a dedicated capital markets capability in place before launch, whether built internally or outsourced to a third-party trading sub-advisor. A competent capital markets team monitors the fund in real time, spots anomalous spread widening or volume spikes the moment they occur, and reaches out to the lead market maker immediately to understand whether there's a basket issue, a halted underlying name, or simply unusual activity. The difference between resolving that kind of issue in seconds versus hours is the difference between running a professional operation and generating advisor questions that erode confidence in the product. As a fund company adds products across different asset classes with different trading characteristics, that internal backbone becomes progressively more important.

Notable Insights

"Volume and assets tend to drive the success of ETFs more so than performance."

"I think one of the most important things to do is just make sure that your points of distribution where you plan on selling the fund—those gateways are open. And sometimes it's simply just adding a symbol and a CUSIP to some master database."

"Having a capital markets team internally, if not outsourcing that to a trading sub-advisor, is an important role. It's worth the investment. It leads to successful launches—but not just launches themselves—day-to-day, week-to-week efficiency."

Key Takeaways

  • GTS serves as lead market maker for 341 US-listed ETFs and trades across more than 3,200 exchange-traded products, with expertise spanning equity, fixed income, and commodity strategies.
  • The NYSE Designated Market Maker model adds a human overlay to algorithmic quoting, producing tighter spreads and better market depth for new fund launches.
  • Pre-launch, issuers must verify that key custodians have the fund's symbol and CUSIP active and ready to accept orders—this step is frequently skipped and costs real business on day one.
  • A dedicated capital markets function is essential for issuers who want to run a scalable, professional operation, whether staffed internally or outsourced.
  • Volume and assets—not investment performance—drive ETF success, which makes operational execution at and around launch disproportionately important.
  • As a firm adds products across different asset classes, the complexity of daily operational monitoring compounds; capital markets infrastructure needs to scale in step with the product lineup.

What This Means for Advisors

For advisors who use ETFs as portfolio building blocks, the mechanics Paul describes are largely invisible—which is exactly how it should work when the infrastructure behind a fund is sound. But understanding what a lead market maker does helps explain why spread, market depth, and exchange structure matter when evaluating a newer fund. Tight spreads don't happen by accident; they reflect deliberate operational decisions made by the issuer and their market making partner before the fund ever opened for trading. When evaluating a smaller or recently launched ETF, the presence of a serious capital markets function in the background is a meaningful indicator of whether that fund is built for the long run.

Listen

Paul Weisbruch's conversation with Brad is a straight look at the operational machinery that makes ETFs function—and what it takes for issuers to build something worth holding. Hear the full episode of Behind the Ticker wherever you listen to podcasts.

title: Kyle Wiggs on Technology-Driven Investing and the Future of the Advisor Tech Stack
metaDescription: Kyle Wiggs, founder of UX Wealth Partners, joins Behind the Ticker to discuss technology-driven investment strategies, advisor platforms, and the future of p...
content:
Kyle Wiggs spent years watching investment advisors struggle with platforms built in the late 1990s and early 2000s, carrying commodity model portfolios, and competing almost entirely on price or relationships. When the broker dealer network he helped run was sold to LPL in 2017, he spent the next several years conducting due diligence on whether the platform advisors actually needed already existed. His conclusion: it did not. That assessment led to the founding of UX Wealth Partners, a turnkey asset management platform built around the premise that technology-driven investment strategies represent the most meaningful differentiation available to advisors today—and that the way portfolios are constructed now will look primitive from the vantage point of ten or fifteen years from now.

About Kyle Wiggs and UX Wealth Partners

Wiggs started his career in the early 2000s as an external wholesaler, spending years inside advisors' offices to understand what they needed and where their platform relationships fell short. He later served on various industry leaders' councils and joined a broker dealer network that served approximately 4,000 advisors nationwide. There, his responsibilities spanned platform technology, trading, billing, reporting, and distribution. When that entity was sold to LPL in 2017, Wiggs took stock of what he had learned and founded UX Wealth Partners in June 2020.

UX Wealth operates as a TAMP—turnkey asset management platform—but with a defining thesis that separates it from the traditional model. Rather than offering the same menu of Vanguard, Russell, and American Funds strategies available on every other platform, UX Wealth sources exclusively or near-exclusively from technology-driven and quantitative managers, holding exclusive agreements with most of them. The technology stack was built with infrastructure support from a professional who previously built Circle Black, and the firm has invested heavily in ensuring that back-end data integration between its components actually functions correctly—which Wiggs views as a more consequential differentiator than any individual feature.

Investment Strategy and Approach

UX Wealth's approach to manager selection begins with one question: what specific, bounded objective is this technology designed to solve? Wiggs used Tesla's autonomous driving challenges as a frame—the problem failed in part because the problem space was too large and too variable to program effectively. The managers on the UX Wealth platform are solving defined, testable problems, and the firm evaluates them on whether their process held up in 2008, in Q4 2018, and during the COVID-19 drawdown.

The manager universe divides into two broad categories. The first is strategies designed to minimize volatility and manage drawdowns using rules-based or quantitative approaches—portfolios that eliminate the human emotional component from de-risking decisions. The second is strategies pursuing alpha through capabilities that are structurally beyond what human analysts can replicate at scale. Wiggs cited one example in detail: a sentiment analysis platform that tracks every piece of digital content published about each of the 500 names in the S&P 500 on a weekly basis, synthesizes that volume into directional sentiment scores, and selects the top 30 names by positive momentum. By the time a human analyst has completed research, presented to an investment committee, and received a vote, the information is already reflected in the price. The technology closes that latency gap.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

Why the Traditional TAMP Model Has Commoditized the Advisory Business

Wiggs's critique of the traditional TAMP market is structural, not competitive. He observed that most broker dealers use the same underlying technology—often built in the late 1990s and early 2000s—and source from the same roster of model managers. The result is that advisors with nearly identical tech stacks and nearly identical investment offerings compete almost entirely on price or personal relationships rather than investment outcomes. He acknowledged that no technology provider walks in and says their product does not work well. The weakness is below the surface: how well do systems communicate on the back end, how accurately does data pass between components, and how much friction exists in that integration layer. Wiggs described this as where UX Wealth spent significant engineering effort and time—and where legacy platforms quietly fall short without ever advertising the fact.

Hub-and-Spoke vs. All-in-One: Why Flexibility Matters More Than Convenience

Wiggs pushed back explicitly against the all-in-one platform movement during the conversation. His argument is practical: committing to an all-in-one solution requires an advisor to accept every component of that stack, including the ones they would not choose independently. More importantly, it forecloses the ability to adopt better-fit technologies as they emerge. An advisor locked into an all-in-one that was best-in-class in 2020 faces a major migration decision the moment something meaningfully better becomes available. His preferred architecture is a hub-and-spoke model: a centralized dashboard that integrates with multiple custodians and communicates accurately with a best-of-breed set of tools—CRM, risk analysis, billing, reporting—that advisors can update incrementally without rebuilding everything at once. He listed multi-custodian connectivity, automated billing, and accurate overnight data processing as the non-negotiable foundation layers; everything else can evolve.

Where AI Belongs—and Where It Does Not

One of the more pointed observations in the conversation involved Wiggs's distinction between AI for investment decisions and AI for client service. He expressed strong conviction that technology-driven investment management is improving—he described it as getting better while human capabilities remain relatively static—and that the trend will continue. But he pushed back firmly on deploying AI as a client-facing service replacement. Clients with meaningful assets, in his experience, want to speak with a person they trust. Advisors with healthy businesses want a support team that picks up the phone. His concern is that some platforms are using AI on the service side primarily to paper over inefficiencies in their own legacy infrastructure—using the technology to mask problems rather than solve them. He was explicit that UX Wealth's position is strong support for AI in investment management and skepticism about AI as a substitute for human contact in the advice relationship.

Notable Insights

"There will come a day where we'll look back and go, 'Remember when humans used to pick stocks and ETFs and mutual funds?' We're not there yet, but that's where we're headed."

"Technology doesn't have an opinion—a bias, if you will—around growth versus value, US versus international, large cap versus small cap. It's simply trying to determine: is the environment we're in today better suited for a value company or a growth company?"

"I don't care if you're the best money manager in the world—in 2008 or during COVID—you became emotional just like everybody else. And so your strategy all of a sudden becomes skewed by that human emotional component."

Key Takeaways

  • UX Wealth Partners sources exclusively from technology-driven and rules-based investment managers, holding exclusive agreements with most of them—manager differentiation is the core platform value proposition
  • Manager selection begins with one question: what specific, bounded objective is this technology designed to solve? Strategies must demonstrate their process held up in major drawdowns, not just favorable conditions
  • The manager universe divides into two categories: drawdown management through rules-based de-risking, and alpha generation through capabilities structurally beyond what human analysts can replicate
  • A hub-and-spoke technology architecture gives advisors more long-term flexibility than all-in-one solutions, which create high switching costs and slow adaptation to emerging technology
  • AI for investment decisions is advancing meaningfully and Wiggs views it as a genuine edge; AI as a client service substitute is, in his view, often a patch for legacy infrastructure problems rather than a genuine improvement
  • The advisor technology stack's most important feature is accurate back-end data integration between components—a dimension that rarely appears in platform marketing but consistently determines day-to-day functionality

What This Means for Advisors

The conversation with Wiggs offers a practical framework for advisors evaluating their technology stack or a TAMP relationship. His argument is not that human judgment has no place in advisory—he is explicit that client-facing advice requires a human component and that clients with real assets want a person they trust. The argument is narrower: within portfolio construction, removing emotional decision-making during market stress and leveraging technology that processes information at a scale no analyst team can match creates a structural edge over time. For advisors evaluating their current platform, the questions Wiggs raises are worth pressure-testing against any provider: How does data actually move between your systems? How many custodians can you connect without disruption to existing clients? What happens to your client experience when you need to change one component of the stack five years from now? The answers matter more than the feature list on the sales deck.

Listen to the full episode to hear Wiggs's detailed breakdown of the advisor technology stack, the manager selection framework at UX Wealth, and what he sees as the next frontier in model portfolio construction.

title: John Davi and Frank Tedesco on Quality, Equal Weight, and the Concentration Problem Defining This Market Cycle
metaDescription: John Davi and Frank Tedesco of Astoria join Behind the Ticker to break down their equal-weight quality US equity strategy and the case against megacap concen...
content:
John Davi and Frank Tedesco of Astoria returned to Behind the Ticker to discuss the firm's second ETF: a quality-focused, equal-weight US equity strategy built to address what they see as one of the most pronounced concentration risks in modern market history. With seven to nine stocks driving the bulk of S&P 500 returns in 2023, Astoria's thesis is that the rest of the US equity market—trading at significantly more attractive valuations—represents a better risk-adjusted opportunity for patient investors. The conversation covered market structure, factor research, portfolio construction mechanics, and the practical reasons Astoria chose active management status over a passive index approach.

About John Davi, Frank Tedesco, and Astoria

John Davi is a recurring guest on Behind the Ticker and a co-founder of Astoria, which had already launched a PPI-linked inflation strategy before adding a quality-focused fund in fall 2023. Davi spent 18 years on a Wall Street trading floor—a background that directly shapes how Astoria thinks about index construction, rebalance timing, and the cost of telegraphing trades in advance. Frank Tedesco joined Astoria in 2020 during the pandemic as an intern from Fordham University. He has been present for both fund launches and has taken on growing responsibility with each one.

Astoria describes itself as "longer term strategic in nature, not tactical." The firm's macro framework—built around the view that real rates should run higher for longer, supported by a resilient consumer, a tight labor market, and above-trend inflation—shapes both the market outlook and the construction logic behind the quality strategy. Astoria's view is not that advisors should abandon megacap tech exposure, but that equal-weight quality can serve as a strategic complement to positions that already have that concentration built in.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The strategy holds 100 US stocks selected for quality across the market cap spectrum—primarily large and midcap names above $5 billion in market value. The quality filter draws on return on equity, return on assets, and return on invested capital, with different metric combinations applied to different sectors based on historical research. The fund starts from all US listings, screens to approximately 800 names based on minimum market cap of $5 billion, minimum free float of 25%, and average daily volume above $50 million over the prior six months. From those 800, sector-specific quality screens identify the final 100 holdings.

The construction is explicitly equal weight. Each of the 100 names receives a 1% allocation at inception, and quarterly rebalances bring individual positions back toward that target. Annual adds and deletes run on a rules-based schedule. Critically, the fund is structured as an actively managed product rather than a passive index. The sector weightings mirror the broader large-cap universe: if information technology represents 28% of the market, the fund carries 28% in technology—but spread across 28 individual names at 1% each, rather than concentrated in three or four megacap positions.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

Why Concentration Risk Is the Defining Problem Right Now

Davi drew an explicit parallel between the current market structure and the late 1990s technology bubble. In 1999, capital flooded into internet stocks, valuations in that cohort drove up index averages, and the rest of the market—often better-valued and more profitable—was ignored. He sees the same dynamic at work in 2023, with the Magnificent 7 serving as the concentration vehicle. His point is not that these are bad businesses. By Astoria's own quality screens, several of them qualify as legitimate quality companies. The problem is the valuation spread between those names and the rest of the US market, which has become historically wide as passive flows and model portfolio allocations compound the effect. He noted that the rest of the US market, excluding those seven names, was trading at roughly 15 to 16 times forward earnings—a level Davi characterized as genuinely attractive—while inclusion of the megacap cohort pushed the index multiple to the 20 to 21 range.

The Active vs. Passive Decision in Construction

Davi's trading floor background produced a specific opinion on why Astoria chose active management status rather than a passive index structure. Published index methodologies create predictable rebalance windows—specific dates, specific closing prices—that sophisticated market participants monitor and use to front-run. An actively managed structure gives Astoria the flexibility to execute rebalances when conditions favor it rather than when a calendar requires it. He also cited the risk of a single name going to zero between scheduled rebalances—referencing Enron and Worldcom specifically—as a reason the team wanted the ability to remove a position outside the annual cycle if a catastrophic event occurred. The fund is rules-based in its selection process but operationally active in its execution.

Quality as the Organizing Principle—and What the Factor Research Actually Shows

Tedesco walked through why quality was chosen as the structural factor over alternatives. The Fama-French factor research shows quality companies have historically produced a higher Sharpe ratio compared to other factor categories—meaning better return per unit of risk accepted, even if quality does not always produce the highest absolute returns. He also identified a structural problem with existing quality ETFs: most are market-cap weighted. That means advisors who believe they have diversified quality exposure may unknowingly be holding 15 to 20% of their quality allocation in the same megacap names they already own in their core passive positions. An equal-weight quality construction eliminates that overlap and creates a genuinely differentiated ballast. On the long-term performance case, both Davi and Tedesco pointed to the S&P equal-weight index's cumulative outperformance versus the market-cap weighted S&P 500 since 1999—a margin they described as approximately 50 to 60%, not a few basis points. Davi noted that even Eric Balchunas of Bloomberg flagged the Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF, a long-standing equal-weight product, has outperformed the S&P 500 over its history.

Notable Insights

"So much money went into passive indexation, so much money went to model portfolios, so many RIAs launched—and it exacerbated the concentration of these seven megacap stocks."

"If you take out those seven stocks, the rest of the US market is actually really attractive—it's on average a 15 to 16 P/E ratio, which is not terrible. Once you include those seven stocks, forward earnings is like 20 to 21."

"The S&P equal-weighted index has outperformed the S&P 500 market cap-weighted index substantially since 1999—it's like 50 to 60 percent, not a few basis points."

Key Takeaways

  • Astoria sees the Magnificent 7 concentration dynamic as structurally similar to the late 1990s tech bubble—the rest of the US equity market, excluding those names, is trading at historically reasonable multiples
  • The strategy holds 100 equal-weight names selected by return on equity, return on assets, and return on invested capital, with sector weights mirroring the broader large-cap index
  • The fund is actively managed rather than indexed specifically to avoid front-running risk associated with published passive rebalance schedules, and to allow for off-cycle position removal in catastrophic single-name events
  • Quality as a factor has historically produced higher Sharpe ratios than other factor categories, providing more predictable risk-adjusted outcomes even when absolute returns lag in momentum-driven markets
  • Many existing quality ETFs are market-cap weighted, which concentrates them in the same megacap names that drive passive index performance—an equal-weight construction eliminates that structural overlap
  • The S&P equal-weight index has outperformed the market-cap weighted S&P 500 by approximately 50 to 60% since 1999; past performance does not guarantee future results, but the long-run data supports the structural argument

What This Means for Advisors

For advisors fielding questions about S&P 500 concentration—or whose existing quality ETF allocations are unknowingly carrying heavy exposure to the same names already held in core passive positions—Astoria's approach offers a structurally different solution. Equal weight combined with a quality screen creates diversified quality exposure that is sector-proportional to the market but not market-cap driven. Davi and Tedesco frame this as a complement to existing growth and megacap positions, not a wholesale replacement. But for the ballast component of a US equity allocation, the construction logic behind equal-weight quality is worth evaluating carefully as the next market cycle begins to take shape. The practical questions worth asking any quality ETF provider: how are the sector weights determined, and what percentage of the portfolio is in the same seven names that already dominate the index?

Listen to the full episode to hear Davi and Tedesco's complete market outlook for Q4 2023 and their framework for thinking about late-cycle equity positioning.

title: Elysabeth Alfano on Food Systems Disruption and the Investment Case for Plant-Based Innovation
metaDescription: Elysabeth Alfano of Veg Tech Invest joins Behind the Ticker to make the business case for plant-based innovation as a major investment opportunity.
content:
Elysabeth Alfano built her career in the heart of the food industry before making one of the more compelling investment arguments in the ETF space. As the founder of Veg Tech Invest and portfolio manager of what she describes as the world's only plant-based Innovation ETF, Alfano brings a practitioner's lens to a sector most investors either dismiss or misunderstand. On this episode of Behind the Ticker, she breaks down why the global food supply system is ripe for disruption—and why that disruption is an investment thesis, not an environmental virtue signal.

About Elysabeth Alfano and Veg Tech Invest

Alfano's path to founding Veg Tech Invest runs directly through the Kellogg Company, where she worked on brands including Special K and Frosted Mini-Wheats. Her role was not limited to brand management—she ran independent businesses covering production, packaging, logistics, shipping, sales, and marketing across international markets. When she shifted her personal diet away from meat and dairy for health reasons, she started researching the structural inefficiencies in the global food supply system. What she found was not a lifestyle story but a business equation badly in need of correction.

She co-founded the fund with Dr. Sasha Goodman, combining backgrounds in food operations and investment research. The fund invests in food innovation companies across the full supply chain—from early agtech and precision fermentation to consumer packaged goods and sustainable materials—with the stated goal of capturing the disruption of a global food system she sees as fundamentally unsustainable on both economic and resource grounds.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The fund's thesis rests on a business argument before anything else: the current food supply system is deeply inefficient, and companies solving that inefficiency represent a durable investment opportunity. Alfano grounds the case in hard numbers. It takes 25 to 35 calories of crop to produce one calorie of beef. Approximately 41% of the world's tropical deforestation comes from animal factories. Animal agriculture accounts for roughly 32% of global methane emissions. Americans were eating approximately 285 to 290 pounds of meat per year as of 2019, a figure that has continued to rise. Meat carries no fiber, induces compounds linked to cardiovascular disease, and in its processed form is classified by the World Health Organization as a Class 1 carcinogen.

The portfolio is constructed along the entire food supply chain. That includes agtech companies working on precision watering and novel crop technologies, ingredient and protein companies, flavor and texture innovators, and consumer packaged goods brands at the end of the line. The fund also carries a materials allocation—approximately 20% of the portfolio—reflecting downstream consequences of a shift away from animal agriculture, including alternative leather derived from agricultural byproducts such as pineapple skins. The fund spans small, mid, and large cap companies across global markets, which Alfano is explicit about: this is not a nanocap or niche play.

She cited research from the Boston Consulting Group showing that investing in plant-based innovation is three to forty times more impactful on greenhouse gas emissions than investing in alternative building materials, electric vehicles, or alternative energy—and requires significantly less capital expenditure to produce that impact, because much of the physical infrastructure for food production already exists.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

The Inefficiency of the Current System—and Who Pays for It

Alfano's most pointed argument is that the food industry currently externalizes massive costs—water, land, methane—and as those costs become priced into regulation, supply chain transparency, and water scarcity, the margins of companies built on those externalities will compress. She draws a line from the calorie conversion problem (25 to 35 calories of crop input for one calorie of beef output) to tropical deforestation, water consumption, and ultimately to the financial sustainability of large food manufacturers. She put the scale in direct terms: there are 80 billion animals in factory conditions globally against a human population of 8 billion, and the waste from those facilities flows into the same water systems communities depend on. That cost is currently not on the income statement. It will be.

Consumer, Government, and Industry Converging at Once

What Alfano argues separates this moment from past food trend cycles is the simultaneous convergence of three distinct pressures. Younger consumers—Millennials, Gen Z, and Gen Alpha—are directing spending toward brands that align with their values. Governments are investing in food IP as a national security matter, having watched supply chains fracture during COVID-19 and during the Ukraine conflict. China, with 1.4 billion people and deep reliance on food imports, is investing heavily in cultivated and alternative proteins precisely because food sovereignty requires it. And large food manufacturers are operating under growing scrutiny from regulators and investors for their environmental footprint. The result, in Alfano's framing, is that industry, government, and consumer demand are all pushing in the same direction simultaneously, for different reasons.

The Protein Efficiency Argument—and What Is Actually Coming to Plates

One of the more counterintuitive observations Alfano shared: the highest protein concentration on the planet does not come from animal sources. It comes from lentils. She uses this to reframe the food innovation story not as a campaign to eliminate meat but as a structural redirect of the protein supply chain toward more efficient sources. Cultivated meat grown from cells in controlled environments, precision fermentation proteins, and plant-derived ingredients are all part of the portfolio. The common thread is efficiency: more nutrition from fewer resources, on less land, with less water. Alfano was direct about the consumer behavior reality: most people are not going vegan, and the fund is not betting on that. The bet is that how meat comes to the consumer will change—not whether people eat it.

Notable Insights

"It takes 25 to 35 calories of crop to get one calorie of beef—so what that means is you deforest and you grow crops that have protein and fiber, do you give that food to people? No, you give it to animals."

"Investing in plant-based innovation is three times to 40 times more impactful than the others—alternative building materials, electric vehicles, alternative energy—because you don't need as much capex to invest in shifting over that system, a lot of the capex already exists."

"We have eight billion people on the planet, 80 billion animals in factories—where do you think they go to the bathroom? It's your water. It's your land and water."

Key Takeaways

  • The investment case for plant-based innovation is grounded in business efficiency, not lifestyle preference—the current food supply system externalizes costs that are increasingly being internalized through regulation and resource scarcity
  • The fund invests across the full food supply chain—from agtech and precision fermentation to consumer packaged goods and sustainable materials—with approximately 80% food and 20% materials exposure
  • The BCG found plant-based innovation to be 3x to 40x more impactful on greenhouse gas emissions than EVs or alternative energy, requiring less capital to achieve that impact
  • Consumer demand, government food IP investment, and regulatory pressure on large food manufacturers are converging simultaneously—this is not a single-driver thesis
  • The fund is not betting on widespread veganism; it is betting that the method of protein delivery will shift toward more efficient systems while total meat consumption continues
  • The global food system is an $8 trillion industry; the meat sector alone is $1.4 trillion—the scale of this disruption opportunity is comparable to any major technology transition

What This Means for Advisors

The plant-based innovation space is routinely dismissed as a niche ESG play, but Alfano's framing makes a different argument. She is not pitching values alignment—she is pitching structural business disruption in a sector with massive scale, converging policy and consumer tailwinds, and companies on the right side of cost curves as water, land, and environmental externalities become more expensive inputs. For advisors building portfolios with a climate-aware or impact allocation, the fund offers global, multi-cap exposure across an entire supply chain rather than a single thematic bet on any one protein source or technology category. The 80/20 food-to-materials split is worth understanding in detail before positioning it relative to other holdings, and the fund's explicit rejection of the niche-vegan framing is useful context for advisor-client conversations about what the strategy actually owns.

Listen to this episode to hear Alfano's full breakdown of precision fermentation, cultivated meat, and what she expects to land on plates within the next five years.

title: Ryan Pannell on Kaiju Advisors: What a Fully AI-Directed ETF Actually Looks Like
metaDescription: Ryan Pannell of Kaiju Advisors joins Behind the Ticker to explain how AI makes investment decisions, the story behind their risk containment system, and the ...
content:
The phrase "AI-powered" has become noise in financial marketing. Ryan Pannell built his career doing the real thing — first in applied cryptography and theoretical physics, then as a quantitative portfolio manager at large hedge funds, and eventually as the architect of trading systems where artificial intelligence makes the endpoint decisions. When he joined Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker, he was one of the most technically credible guests the show has featured: someone who can explain how machine learning actually makes investment decisions, why guardrails matter more than gross returns, and what happened when one of his early systems went catastrophically wrong in a single month and cost the team nearly everything it had made in the previous five.

About Ryan Pannell and Kaiju Advisors

Ryan Pannell is the founder of Kaiju ETF Advisors, an SEC-registered investment adviser, and Kaiju Capital Management, a BVI-based approved manager that runs four fully subscribed private funds. His background spans applied cryptography, theoretical physics, equity trading, and portfolio management at large hedge funds before he launched his own funds. About half a decade before the episode recorded, Pannell began applying artificial intelligence to investment management on the private fund side — building on earlier experience using AI in limited capacities during cryptography work to support random number generation protocols. The private fund results were strong enough that he and his partners began investigating whether certain strategies could be converted into publicly accessible ETFs.

The strategy that fit best for market makers and the broader public market was a low-to-high mean reversion approach — what Pannell describes plainly as a dip buying strategy. The ETF launched in December 2022 on the New York Stock Exchange and became one of only six ETFs operating on the NYSE floor. A few months before the episode, Citadel helped bring the fund down to the floor, tightening spreads to two to three cents. The practical impact was direct: before the move, spreads of eight to twelve cents on ARCA meant retail investors placing market orders — especially in the first five minutes of trading — could receive significantly disadvantageous fills. Pannell had watched it happen. The floor presence resolved it.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The strategy is a low-to-high mean reversion system: the AI identifies what it determines to be artificially oversold stocks and takes positions anticipating a reversion toward fair value. That description is deliberately accessible; the underlying machinery is more involved. Kaiju's approach starts with a human-developed trading ideology that is taught to the AI system. The machine is given a defined strategy, explicit guardrails — position sizing limits, aggregate risk thresholds, strategy constraints such as remaining long-only equity — and asked to optimize within those parameters. It does. And sometimes it proposes refinements, adjustments to the approach that the machine believes would improve the projected outcome, which the human team evaluates before accepting.

This is not a pure blackbox system. Pannell draws a sharp distinction between systems where the machine's decisions are genuinely opaque and his approach, where the investment ideology is human-originated and the machine's refinements operate within a structure the team understands. Pure blackbox AI systems can generate extraordinary multi-year returns — but those results typically include two or three periods of 70% to 90% drawdowns, which makes them unsuitable for client capital regardless of disclosed performance numbers. The fund was up approximately 12% in the 90 days preceding the conversation.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

The Story of TJ

Pannell's most instructive lesson came before Kaiju's current systems were operational. In 2019, the team built an early, less sophisticated iteration of their AI system. It worked brilliantly: five straight months of returns so strong that the team debated whether to mention them publicly at all. "The performance was so outrageous that we weren't mentioning it to anyone because we thought they wouldn't believe it." In the sixth month, the system failed to identify a deep sector rotation and lost nearly everything it had made in the prior five months in a single trade. The system had a name — TJ. "TJ basically got drunk and drove off a cliff."

The failure was survivable because the team was still ahead in aggregate. But it exposed a fundamental flaw in the design: the guardrails were set far too wide from actual positions, and the system had been optimized entirely for gross return with no countervailing risk architecture. That experience led directly to the development of the ARC system — the AI Risk Containment framework — which now functions as a dedicated AI offramp for risk management, running as a separate layer alongside the core trading AI. "I'm so grateful for that," Pannell reflects, "because it wasn't catastrophic — and it really paved the road to where we have gotten to now."

How AI Makes Decisions — And Why It Is Like Liking Art

Pannell offers a useful explanation of how AI investment decisions differ from human ones. Human investors follow a linear process: a criterion flags, further evaluation happens, a decision is reached. AI does not work that way. Instead, it makes decisions the way a person instantly likes a piece of art or finds someone attractive — a non-linear process that arrives at a conclusion without an articulable step-by-step path. "If I stopped you in the moment and said 'really, why?' it would be hard for you to articulate that. Your brain knew it instantly. You didn't need to do any further calculation." Humans make investing decisions linearly because they have to; AI does not have that constraint. Blackbox systems represent the extreme version — decisions that genuinely cannot be explained. Kaiju's approach tries to stay inside the explainable range by building from a human ideology and constraining the machine's degrees of freedom within that framework.

AI's Role in Financial Advice

Pannell has a considered view of how AI will affect the advisory business, and it is more nuanced than either the catastrophizing or the dismissing camps. His core argument: AI is genuinely bad at context. It cannot distinguish between what a client says they can tolerate and what they actually can. It cannot push back when an investor with significant existing debt expresses a desire for maximum risk and says they do not care if they lose everything. That challenge function — where the advisor's experience translates expressed preferences into real objectives — requires a human. He does expect AI-assisted diagnostic tools to emerge, helping advisors quickly model potential allocation options for a given client profile. But the advisor, like the doctor using AI for diagnostics, still has to be in the room gathering the information. He does allow that advisors operating at the very bottom end of the quality spectrum may face displacement — but characterizes that as a small cohort within the broader professional advisory space.

Notable Insights

"AI can't do any of that. It's terrible at context. It doesn't understand nuance. It's really great at smashing numbers. So I don't see it taking the advisory business away. I see it being a tool that RAs can use to get to answers more quickly."

"There's a trading strategy which is developed by humans. We've used it before, it's made us money. We teach the machine. Then there's this refinement period that we go through where within the guardrails we place — we tell it what it's trying to do with what we give it, and then we ask it for optimization output."

"I don't think I will ever forget that iteration of the system. We gave it a name. It was TJ. And TJ basically got drunk and drove off a cliff. I'm so grateful for that because it wasn't catastrophic — and it really paved the road to where we have gotten to now."

Key Takeaways

  • The fund is a fully AI-directed low-to-high mean reversion strategy that identifies artificially oversold stocks — all decisions at the endpoint are made by the AI, not a human portfolio manager
  • Kaiju's approach starts with a human-built investment ideology that is taught to the machine, with explicit guardrails on position sizing, risk, and strategy scope — it is not a pure blackbox system
  • The ARC (AI Risk Containment) system functions as a dedicated risk management layer running separately from the core trading AI, developed after an early system generated severe losses in a single month following five strong months of returns
  • The fund is one of six ETFs operating on the NYSE floor; Citadel's involvement tightened spreads to two to three cents, directly improving execution quality for retail investors placing market orders
  • Pannell views AI as a tool for financial advisors, not a replacement — AI cannot navigate the context gap between what clients express and what they actually need
  • Pure blackbox AI systems typically pair extraordinary multi-year returns with 70% to 90% drawdown periods — a risk profile Pannell considers incompatible with client capital regardless of disclosed performance

What This Means for Advisors

The fund represents something genuinely uncommon: a publicly accessible, exchange-traded vehicle where investment decisions are made entirely by artificial intelligence, built by people with deep backgrounds in AI systems rather than financial marketing. For advisors interested in alternative-style, systematic strategies with explicit risk architecture, the fund offers a transparent description of how the system works, why the guardrails exist, and what happens when they do not. Pannell's commentary on AI and the advisory business is also worth the listen on its own terms — his argument that AI will augment rather than replace professional advisors is grounded in specific system limitations he has observed firsthand, not in general reassurance. Advisors fielding client questions about AI in portfolio management will find his framework for explaining the difference between what AI does well and what it cannot do particularly useful.

The full conversation with Ryan Pannell is available on Behind the Ticker. It covers the complete architecture of the AI system, the failure of TJ and what it taught the team about risk containment, the mechanics of floor-based ETF trading and why it matters for retail execution, and Pannell's detailed views on how artificial intelligence will reshape — but not replace — the financial advisory profession.

title: Alejandro Garza on Aseam's Rules-Based Strategy: Six Factors, 27 Stocks, and a Global Developed Markets Edge
metaDescription: Alejandro Garza of Aseam joins Behind the Ticker to walk through the six-factor model behind AZTD, its 27-stock portfolio construction, and why global develo...
content:
Most fund managers claim their process blends quantitative rigor with fundamental insight. Alejandro Garza built a firm specifically to make that combination work — not as marketing language, but as operational architecture. Garza is the founder and portfolio manager at Aseam, and when he joined Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker, he walked through exactly how a six-factor proprietary model selects just 27 stocks from a universe of roughly 4,000 companies across the global developed markets small and midcap space. The methodology is specific, the construction is deliberate, and the reasoning behind every design decision is traceable to a real problem the team set out to solve.

About Alejandro Garza and Aseam

Garza launched Aseam in 2016 after more than 20 years of experience in corporate finance, investment banking, and emerging markets asset management. He spent ten years at the Ashmore Group and prior to that worked at Emerging Markets Management, one of the pioneers of EM investing, based in Washington DC. When he started the firm, he had a specific thesis: combining quantitative analysis with fundamental analysis, applied to a concentrated portfolio of 15 to 20 names, was the best way to generate client returns — and it was structurally difficult to execute inside a large institution.

The firm name carries meaning. Aseam derives from the Nahuatl language — the original tongue of the Aztec people — and translates roughly as "place of heron." Garza is the Spanish word for heron, so the name connects the firm's Mexican heritage to Alejandro's own family name. "I wanted a name that conveyed Mexican origin and tradition," he explains, noting that he also wanted to distinguish Aseam from the English-named competitors that invest in Latin America without that heritage.

Today Aseam operates two business lines: an active SMA strategy where the team conducts full fundamental due diligence and meets directly with company management, and a rules-based ETF that serves as the quantitative, index-based version of that same process. The client base is evenly split between Mexico and the United States. The ETF was cross-listed in Mexico specifically because Mexican regulations do not permit actively managed ETFs from being listed there, and because listed ETFs benefit from a significant tax advantage — roughly 10% versus 30% or more on standard investment returns.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The strategy targets global developed markets small and midcap companies — defined as those with market capitalizations between $500 million and $10 billion — across three regions: North America (the US and Canada), Western Europe (including the UK and the Nordics), and Developed Asia (Japan, Australia, and New Zealand). Within each region, the model evaluates companies across nine of the eleven standard GICS sectors. Energy is excluded because of commodity-driven volatility. Real estate is excluded because many clients already carry direct real estate exposure in their portfolios. Pharmaceuticals and biotech are excluded as an industry due to the event-driven volatility that disrupts systematic stock selection in concentrated portfolios.

From the roughly 4,000 companies in that investable universe, the model runs each through a six-factor scoring system. Every factor is scored from zero to one — one representing the highest rank — and each factor is equally weighted at one-sixth of the total score. The six factors are: free cash flow (including yield, margin, and trend), return on equity, earnings growth (including top-line revenue growth), earnings revisions, value (price-to-earnings and earnings yield), and momentum (stock price action). The model then selects the single highest-ranked stock in each of the 27 region-sector combinations — three regions times nine sectors — producing a 27-stock equally weighted portfolio.

The index rebalances monthly. Garza chose monthly rebalancing deliberately, noting it differs from the typical quarterly or annual schedule of most rules-based products. Because the strategy is always targeting the top-ranked stock in each cell of a 27-cell matrix, maintaining those factor exposures requires frequent updating.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

Why Equal Weighting Won

In designing the portfolio construction methodology, Garza's team ran multiple iterations and back-tested numerous weighting schemes. The conclusion was counterintuitive: the simplest solution performed best. An equal weight across all 27 holdings systematically diversifies risk across regions and sectors simultaneously, without introducing the optimization artifacts that more complex weighting schemes can produce. "It's funny how, especially those of us who work in quantitative finance, sometimes and often the simplest solution ends up being the most effective," Garza notes. "We tend to overthink things and want to optimize everything, but it tends to be the simpler, the better."

The Connection Between the Index and the Active Strategy

The rules-based ETF and the active SMA business are not parallel operations that happen to share a name — they feed each other. When the monthly rebalancing process surfaces a stock that has held the number-one ranking in its region-sector cell across multiple periods, that name becomes a candidate for full fundamental due diligence in the active strategy. The team then meets with management, visits the company, and conducts the deeper analysis the rules-based model cannot capture on its own. "There's a very nice communication between the index rules-based ETF strategy and our stock-picking active strategy," Garza explains. The ETF serves as a continuously updated screen; the active strategy is where the team acts on the most persistent signals from that screen.

Why the Small and Midcap Space

Garza makes a specific case for why companies in the $500 million to $10 billion range in developed markets represent a genuine opportunity. Companies of that size tend to be under-followed by sell-side analysts and under-represented in institutional portfolios relative to mega-cap names. That limited coverage creates a valuation advantage — the market has simply done less work on these names. The global developed markets filter adds another layer: most client portfolios are heavily weighted toward US large-cap equities, leaving international small and midcap companies genuinely underexplored. The unusual combination of global coverage and concentrated selection is precisely what Garza's client base had been asking for — they had US exposure covered by indexed products, but no systematic, stock-selection approach to the rest of the developed world at the smaller end of the market cap spectrum.

Notable Insights

"Combining quantitative analysis with fundamental analysis — I think the combination of those two ways of approaching markets and investments really provides for a competitive advantage when you combine those two aspects in an efficient way."

"We apply the model to the entire roughly 4,000 stock investable universe in the global developed markets small and midcap space. We select the number one stock in each region-sector combination. We end up with a 27-stock portfolio with those 27 selections."

"Aseam literally means 'place of heron.' Heron in Spanish is 'garza,' and that's my last name. So I was able to fit in the family legacy with the Mexican origin."

Key Takeaways

  • The strategy selects exactly 27 stocks — the top-ranked company in each of 27 region-sector combinations — from an investable universe of approximately 4,000 global developed markets small and midcap companies
  • The six-factor model scores free cash flow, return on equity, earnings growth, earnings revisions, valuation, and momentum — all equally weighted
  • Energy, real estate, and pharmaceuticals/biotech are excluded from the investable universe for structural reasons specific to concentrated, systematic strategies
  • Monthly rebalancing keeps factor exposures current in a highly concentrated portfolio — a deliberate departure from the quarterly and annual schedules typical of rules-based products
  • The rules-based ETF and the active SMA strategy share the same underlying model, with persistent index signals feeding candidates into the active due diligence pipeline
  • The investor base is 50% US and 50% Mexico; cross-listing in Mexico provides significant tax advantages for local investors and reflects the firm's core client base

What This Means for Advisors

The strategy gives advisors something specific: a concentrated, rules-based approach to a part of the market — global developed markets small and midcap — that most client portfolios underweight significantly. The 27-stock construction means genuine concentration and potential for meaningful differentiation from broad index exposure. For advisors who want systematic international small and midcap exposure with a clear, explainable process, Garza's methodology connects quantitative scoring directly to fundamental logic in a way that holds up in client conversations. The equal weighting across regions and sectors also provides structural diversification without relying on market-cap concentration, which is a meaningful distinction from many global ETFs that end up dominated by US mega-cap names.

The full episode with Alejandro Garza is available on Behind the Ticker. It includes a detailed walkthrough of the factor model construction, the portfolio weighting methodology, and Garza's perspective on why global developed markets small and midcap companies represent a structurally underexplored source of potential returns for advisors building globally diversified client allocations.

title: David Schulhof on MUSQ: Building the First Pure Play Music ETF
metaDescription: David Schulhof, CEO of MUSQ, joins Behind the Ticker to discuss the first pure play music ETF, its 48-company index, and the forces driving music industry gr...
content:
David Schulhof has spent his entire career inside the music industry — not as an investor looking in, but as an operator, attorney, and builder who has rolled up catalogs, run public companies, and structured deals at the highest levels of the business. That background is precisely why his exchange-traded product, MUSQ, is unlike anything else in the market. When Schulhof joined Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker, the conversation covered everything from the mechanics of the index to the forces reshaping how music is consumed, monetized, and valued globally.

About David Schulhof and MUSQ

David Schulhof is the CEO and founder of MUSQ, the first pure play music ETF. His career reads more like a Hollywood biography than a fund manager's resume. After graduating from Georgetown University early, Schulhof's first job was with Jimmy Iovine at InScope Records — the same Jimmy Iovine who would later co-found Beats with Dr. Dre and sell the company to Apple for $3 billion. Schulhof went on to earn his law degree and was recruited by Disney to serve as music attorney at Miramax and Dimension Films, a role he held for seven years. During that time he built relationships across the entire music ecosystem: songwriters, publishers, labels, and concert promoters.

From there he launched Evergreen, a private equity-backed company that raised $100 million from Lehman Brothers to roll up music publishing, merchandise, and recorded music assets. The portfolio included everything from Billman Row to Death Row Records and spanned bluegrass, hip-hop, rock, and jazz catalogs. That company was sold to KKR and became BMG Rights Management. Schulhof later served as president of two additional music companies, including Live One — a public company that owned Slacker Radio and Podcast One — and was sponsor and board member on two SPAC vehicles before launching MUSQ.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The case for MUSQ rests on a clear observation: music is as fundamental to human life as food and water. Schulhof describes the fund as a non-correlated investment that gives investors broad public exposure to the entire music ecosystem through a rules-based, quarterly-rebalancing index.

The index includes 48 companies organized across five subcategories: streaming (roughly 35% of the portfolio, including the largest global platforms and regional services), content (major labels, K-pop conglomerates, and royalty trusts), live music and ticketing (global concert and ticketing operators), equipment and technology (instrument makers and production tools), and radio and broadcasting (satellite and terrestrial operators). Forty-five percent of holdings are domestic; 55% are international — giving retail investors access to companies they would otherwise struggle to purchase directly.

To qualify for inclusion, a company must meet at least one of three criteria: generate at least 50% of its revenues from music, rank as a top five player in one of the five subcategories, or control more than 10% of global market share in any category. The minimum market cap is $100 million and the minimum daily trading volume is $500,000. Companies with volume between $200,000 and $500,000 receive a half weight. The largest names — major technology platforms that qualify as top five streaming players — are capped at 7% individually. The index rebalances quarterly.

Deeper Dive: Insights from the Full Conversation

AI as Complement, Not Threat

Schulhof is emphatic that artificial intelligence is a complement to the music industry, not a disruption. He draws on the historical pattern every decade or so brings a technology that appears threatening: compact discs, which ultimately doubled the industry; Napster, which gave way to paid streaming platforms. AI, he argues, follows the same arc. He points to Paul McCartney's recent work using AI to produce a track featuring John Lennon's voice, and notes that approximately 120,000 new songs are uploaded to platforms every single day. Rather than undercutting the industry, that volume reinforces the value of labels as curators — someone still has to find the good ones. And when AI-generated content crosses into forgery or piracy, the takedown infrastructure already exists: a fake Drake track was removed from Spotify within hours of a label complaint.

The K-Pop Factor

One of the more surprising dimensions of the index is its substantial exposure to K-pop companies. Schulhof describes how global content creation has democratized music consumption, with every country now hosting its own streaming services and local superstars. The index includes roughly ten K-pop companies — Hybe, JYP, SM Entertainment, Cube, and others — representing significant weight in the content subcategory. He puts it plainly: every country in the world now has some music service, whether it is cloud music in China, regional platforms in Japan or Korea, or growing markets in India. That global content creation is roughly 70% of the companies on the index.

Why the Index Beats Stock Picking Here

Schulhof makes a pointed case for why passive index exposure to music outperforms individual stock selection. An investor trying to build their own music portfolio faces an impossible choice: do you buy a live music and ticketing operator because tour revenues are up, or a major label because a platinum artist is on their roster? The index spreads that selection risk across the full value chain — streaming, content, live music, equipment, and radio — in a single vehicle. For the self-directed investor, the fund also provides access to international companies that would otherwise require opening foreign custody accounts and navigating complex purchase processes.

Notable Insights

"This is a non-correlated investment and it's as important in life as food and water. I don't think a human being can go through life without listening to music in some way. You celebrate music in good times at your weddings and your proms, and you use music in unfortunate times like COVID."

"Goldman Sachs just reported that the industry was going to double to $131 billion by 2030. A lot of that is driven by the top streaming companies like Spotify, Google, and Apple."

"Here we are, ten or fifteen years later with AI. What AI is doing is giving creators new tools to create new music. It's giving an opportunity to even the most amateur musician to create music. I think the benefits far exceed the threats or the costs associated with it."

Key Takeaways

  • MUSQ is the first pure play music ETF, offering exposure to 48 companies across five subcategories of the global music ecosystem
  • The index is 45% domestic and 55% international, providing access to global music companies otherwise difficult for retail investors to purchase directly
  • Inclusion criteria require that companies derive at least 50% of revenues from music, rank as a top five player in a subcategory, or hold more than 10% global market share in any category
  • Goldman Sachs projects the music industry will double to $131 billion by 2030, driven primarily by streaming growth across global markets
  • K-pop represents a significant and growing component of the index, with roughly ten companies included across content subcategories
  • Schulhof views AI as historically consistent with prior technological disruptions — each one appeared threatening and ultimately expanded the industry

What This Means for Advisors

MUSQ presents an unusual combination: thematic growth exposure and genuine diversification within that theme. Unlike sector funds concentrated in a handful of mega-caps, the index spreads across the full music value chain — from streaming platforms to instrument manufacturers to concert promoters — in a globally diversified package. For advisors building thematic allocations, it offers non-correlated exposure to an industry Schulhof argues is structurally growing regardless of the economic cycle. The product is also accessible at any account size, from a first-time retail investor to an institutional allocator seeking a dedicated music allocation. At the time of recording, the MUSQ index was up 22% for the year, outpacing the S&P 500 — a performance narrative advisors can anchor the conversation around.

The full episode with David Schulhof is available on Behind the Ticker. It covers the complete mechanics of the index methodology, Schulhof's firsthand account of building and selling what became BMG Rights Management, and his outlook on how AI and short-form video are reshaping music consumption and monetization over the next decade.

title: Adam Eagleston on Building a Single-Ticket Alternative and the OODA Loop Approach to Portfolio Construction
metaDescription: Adam Eagleston of Formidable walks through the Formidable Fund’s 11-sleeve alternative structure, top-down regime framework, and dynamic options overlay.
content:
Adam Eagleston, chief investment officer at Formidable, joined Brad Roth on Behind the Ticker to walk through the architecture of the Formidable Fund—a single-ticket alternative ETF with eleven sleeves, a top-down and bottom-up construction process, and an options overlay designed to cut off tail risk in severe market events. He also covered why he borrowed a decision-making framework from a fighter pilot, and how to think about a strategy that worked well in 2022 and struggled through 2023 for the same reasons.

About Adam Eagleston and Formidable

Eagleston’s path was shaped early by an unlikely start. He won a stock market competition in sixth grade and built his career around markets from there—despite coming from a family of teachers with no finance background to lean on. After working at a CPA firm out of college, he became the CIO of a large trust company in Memphis, where he ran due diligence on asset managers running separate accounts across value, growth, quant, and other strategies. The experience gave him a vantage point few CIOs have: seeing what works and what does not across a full range of approaches, before ever being in the seat where daily P&L accountability is yours.

In 2012, Eagleston moved to Opus Capital in Cincinnati, a quantamental shop where he developed a small-cap dividend strategy that became foundational to the bottom-up framework he still uses today. A sub-advised ETF launched in 2018 ended when Opus restructured; Eagleston resigned, ran into Formidable CEO Will Brown while clearing out his desk, and was hired before leaving the building. He joined a few months before COVID hit. The firm’s private fund, running since 2016, navigated 2020 well through hedging already in place. That performance validated the approach for a broader launch, and the Formidable Fund came to market in 2021.

Formidable is structured as a hub-and-spoke model with compliance, operations, CFO function, and investments at the hub, and six to eight financial advisors plugging in around it.

Investment Strategy and Approach

The Formidable Fund was designed as a single-ticket alternative solution. The problem it solves: advisors building out an alternatives allocation often hold a dozen small positions—a percent and a half in REITs, 2% in listed private equity, 2% in merger arbitrage—creating administrative overhead and reliance on passively constructed ETFs that rarely hold up to scrutiny. Eagleston consolidates those sleeves into one vehicle with active management, dynamic weighting, and an options overlay.

The fund holds eleven sleeves, including merger arbitrage, listed private equity, royalties, metals and mining, and commodity-sensitive equities, among others. Construction is both top-down and bottom-up. The top-down process identifies which of four broad macro regimes the market is in—modeled after risk parity thinking—using seven factors: inflation, GDP growth, Federal Reserve policy, interest rates, credit spreads, stock multiples, and earnings growth. Each factor is placed on a continuum from negative to positive, and the combined reading informs how aggressively or defensively the fund is weighted across sleeves. In risk-on environments, higher-beta and inflation-sensitive positions are amplified. In risk-off, the fund shifts toward more defensive positioning. The top-down process runs through an investment committee monthly, with Eagleston and Will Brown making final decisions.

The bottom-up process focuses on three components of return: yield, growth, and multiple expansion. Research using Shiller’s data back to 1900 showed multiple expansion is a decade-dependent phenomenon, making dividend yield and earnings growth the more durable sources of long-run returns. Most holdings are identified through screens for dividend-paying companies with credible growth prospects.

Deeper Dive

The Options Overlay: Hedging and Exit Strategy Together

One of the fund’s structural differentiators is an options overlay that serves two purposes simultaneously. The primary hedge uses put options on a high-yield credit ETF. Eagleston’s rationale: the correlation between high-yield credit and equities during risk-off periods tends to be high, but implied volatility on those instruments is materially lower than on equity index options—meaning the same hedge costs less. In more severe risk-off environments, the fund adds put spreads on a small-cap index ETF, which Eagleston described as a belt-and-suspenders approach layered on top of the credit hedge.

The overlay also supports individual position management. The fund writes covered calls on names approaching price targets—when implied volatility is elevated and the position is near fair value, writing a call either harvests premium or results in the stock being called away at the target, a systematic exit without a discrete sell decision.

The OODA Loop Applied to Monthly Portfolio Decisions

The fund’s fact sheet references the OODA Loop—a framework developed by fighter pilot and military strategist John Boyd. Observe, orient, decide, act. Boyd designed it to describe how faster decision cycles outcompete slower ones in combat. Eagleston applies it to the monthly top-down process: the seven macro factors are the observation layer, rates of change drive orientation, sleeve weight decisions follow, and implementation is the act. The premise is that a smaller firm can complete this cycle faster than institutions constrained by size and committee overhead. Getting through the loop faster is a genuine edge when conditions are shifting quickly.

When the Framework Gets Tested: 2022 Versus 2023

The fund’s seven macro factors were predominantly in the negative camp heading into 2022, driving defensive positioning that worked well as markets sold off. In 2023, the same indicators stayed in negative territory but markets diverged from the historical relationships underlying the model—animal spirits in large-cap technology pushed returns in a direction the factor readings did not support. Eagleston was direct about it: the factors that drove 2022 results did not translate to 2023. His response was to hold conviction in the framework rather than adjust to short-term behavior. The distinction between a process working correctly and a cycle not favoring it is central to how he thinks about attribution.

Notable Insights

“We will build something if we think we can do it better. That was really Will’s impetus in building the firm, and it’s what prompted us to launch additional funds.”

“We wanted to have the ability to cut off the tail end of the distribution when you have market events like you had in 2020. To a lesser degree, like you had in 2022.”

“You think about the historical correlation among asset classes and if you were to just have a time machine and say this is what’s going to happen, what do you think would do well, you could very easily make that case anchored on those historical correlations. Which is why we prefer to use hedges—because those are mathematically a more assured way to do it. Now there is a cost associated with that, and we’re dynamic in terms of how we implement the hedges.”

Key Takeaways

  • The Formidable Fund consolidates multiple alternative sleeves into a single vehicle, solving the administrative burden and passive construction quality issues advisors encounter when building an alternatives allocation piecemeal
  • A seven-factor, four-regime top-down framework determines how aggressively or defensively the fund is weighted; the process is monthly, committee-based, with Eagleston and CEO Will Brown making final calls
  • The options overlay serves dual purposes: macro-level tail hedging through credit and small-cap put options, and individual position management through covered call writing for income and exits
  • The OODA Loop provides the operational cadence for top-down decision-making; the advantage is faster cycle time through smaller-firm nimbleness relative to larger institutions
  • Bottom-up security selection anchors on yield and earnings growth as the durable components of long-run equity returns, treating multiple expansion as a cyclical bonus rather than a base case
  • 2022 validated the defensive framework; 2023 tested it against animal spirits in large-cap growth—Eagleston’s response was to stay convicted in the process rather than adjust to short-term divergence

What This Means for Advisors

The Formidable Fund addresses a practical problem in alternatives allocation: small position sizes, passive construction quality, and administrative overhead make the traditional multi-sleeve approach inefficient. A single-ticket structure with active management and a built-in hedging overlay is a legitimate architectural alternative—assuming the top-down process holds up over a full cycle. The options overlay is also worth attention: the use of high-yield credit instruments as a hedge proxy—rather than equity index puts—reflects a cost-of-carry calculation that, when correlation holds, delivers more protection per dollar. The caveat Eagleston acknowledged is that 2022 and 2023 together illustrate the limits of historical factor correlation. The framework is systematic; the market does not always cooperate. Advisors should understand the difference between a process working correctly and a cycle not favoring it—they are two different conversations to have with clients.

Listen to the full conversation with Adam Eagleston on Behind the Ticker, available wherever you get your podcasts.

title: Neil Azous on Goals-Based Investing, Long-Short Equity, and Why Upside Capture Is Non-Negotiable
metaDescription: Neil Azous of Rareview Capital breaks down goals-based investing, why upside capture matters equally, and how his long-short ETF is built.
content:
Neil Azous founded Rareview Capital after building his background at Goldman Sachs, Donaldson Lufkin & Jenrette, and UBS Investment Bank—and he runs his firm around a philosophy that challenges the standard advisory model: that portfolios should be organized around goals, not risk tolerances. On this episode of Behind the Ticker, Azous explains how Rareview builds model portfolios, why their long-short equity strategy is designed to capture upside as aggressively as it protects on the downside, and what he has learned about the economics of the TAMP distribution channel after years of working within it.

About Neil Azous and Rareview Capital
